Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (short):
- Narrow the docinfo_re so it only matches docinfo field lines that have a field name followed by a colon AND whitespace (i.e. ":fieldname: value" or at least ":fieldname: ").
- Replace:
    docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')
  with something like:
    docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s')
  (or r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s.*' — the important part is requiring whitespace after the second colon).

Why this fixes the bug:
- Role/domain markup like ":mod:`mypkg`" does not have a space immediately after the second colon, so it will no longer match docinfo_re. prepend_prolog will not treat it as a docinfo line and will instead insert the prolog at the top of the document (before the role/title), preserving the adjacency between heading text and underline and letting docutils detect the top-level title correctly.

Concretely, the code change (in sphinx/util/rst.py) is:

Change (current):
- docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')  # line 28

To:
- docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s')

(Use the Python raw-string to make the regex readable and anchor to beginning of line.)

Now the required challenge questions (4a–4e):

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current code assumption: Any line that begins with colon + word + colon is a docinfo field (metadata), so treat it as docinfo. New assumption: A docinfo field must have whitespace (a space or other whitespace) after the second colon (i.e. it must be ":fieldname: value"), so lines like ":mod:`...`" (with no whitespace after the second colon) are not docinfo.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The hardcoded value encoding the assumption is docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?'). It encodes "colon, then word characters, then colon, then anything" including roles.
- After the change, the new regex encodes the assumption that docinfo lines require whitespace after the second colon (':fieldname: '). For completeness, I reviewed other hardcoded constants in the same module:
  - SECTIONING_CHARS = ['=', '-', '~']: unrelated.
  - symbols_re and WIDECHARS: unrelated to docinfo parsing.
- Is the new regex still correct for all code paths:
  - All existing tests that expect docinfo handling (test_prepend_prolog) used docinfo lines like ':title: test of SphinxFileInput' which have a space after the second colon; they will still match.
  - Domain roles and inline roles like ':mod:`...`' will not be matched; that addresses the bug.
  - If a user wrote a docinfo line with no space after the second colon (e.g. ':author:Name'), that would not be recognized as docinfo (but such formatting is nonstandard — docutils expects a space). So this change is aligned with the documented docinfo syntax.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary / reverse operations to inserting prolog are parsing and i18n-related re-parsing of single-line messages (sphinx/transforms/i18n.py uses publish_msgstr which clears rst_prolog while reading).
- Relevant parent methods:
  - RSTParser.parse calls decorate (which calls prepend_prolog) and then runs the docutils RSTStateMachine.
- I reviewed sphinx/transforms/i18n.py publish_msgstr (lines 46–80): it explicitly clears rst_prolog before calling the reader to parse translated strings. So i18n correctly avoids applying the prolog in situations where it would confuse small snippets.
- Concrete substitution / trace:
  - With the fix, if the document's first line is ':mod:`mypkg`' and rst_prolog is set, then:
    - Before: docinfo_re.match(':mod:`mypkg`') -> True => pos = 1 => prolog inserted AFTER that line; blank inserted; docutils sees a blank line before the underline/title and fails to treat the top line as a section title. Title not created.
    - After: docinfo_re.match(':mod:`mypkg`') -> False => pos remains 0 => prolog inserted at top (before the role/title) with a trailing blank line inserted after the prolog; the original first lines remain contiguous (role + underline), so docutils will detect the heading correctly.
  - There is no complementary writing operation; this is purely preprocessing of content before parsing. The reverse (post-parse) is unaffected.
- i18n: It already clears rst_prolog for the translated-string parsing path, so no change needed there.

Conclusion for 4c: No parent method performing a complementary operation needs overriding. The parent's behavior will work correctly with the new docinfo detection.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods in sphinx/util/rst.py:
  - escape: unaffected.
  - textwidth, heading: unaffected.
  - default_role: unaffected.
  - prepend_prolog: behavior is adjusted to only treat true docinfo lines as docinfo. Existing tests that expect prolog inserted after docinfo (when there is a proper ":title: ..." with space) will still pass.
  - append_epilog: unaffected.
- Methods in RSTParser (decorate and parse): no changes required. They still call prepend_prolog, which will behave correctly.
- Tests that exercised prepend_prolog with docinfo lines containing a space (tests/test_util_rst.py:test_prepend_prolog) will still pass because those lines still match the new docinfo_re.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new method is required. The only change is to the docinfo_re regex. No overriding of parent methods is needed. I considered whether we needed to change RSTParser.decorate to use a different detection strategy; it is not necessary — adjusting docinfo_re alone addresses the root cause.

Other locations to check:

Checkpoint 5: Final report (root cause, all affected locations, and complete fix)

Root cause:
- The module-level regex docinfo_re in sphinx/util/rst.py is overly broad. It treated any line beginning with ":word:" as docinfo — including domain roles/inline roles such as ":mod:`mypkg`" — and prepend_prolog used that to detect docinfo and insert the rst_prolog after those lines. That insertion plus inserted blank lines broke docutils section-title detection for files whose first heading text was a role expression.

Every location that needs to change:

     - Replace the regex with one that only matches field docinfo lines that have whitespace after the second colon:
       docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s')
       (This requires a space after the second colon, i.e. ":field: value".)

   - Rationale:
     - Docinfo field lines in reST are of the form ":fieldname: value" — having whitespace after the second colon. Inline roles like ":mod:`name`" do not have whitespace after the second colon. Requiring the whitespace prevents roles from being mistaken for docinfo.

- This is the only place in the codebase where docinfo_re is defined and used. The parsing pipeline (RSTParser.decorate -> prepend_prolog) will now behave correctly for all intended docinfo cases and will no longer treat inline roles as docinfo. Existing tests that already used docinfo lines with a trailing space will continue to pass. i18n code already takes care to clear rst_prolog where that is necessary, so no further changes are necessary.

- File: sphinx/util/rst.py

  With:

compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: rst_prolog + a leading role/domain text (e.g. ":mod:`name`") causes Sphinx to drop or mis-parse the document title. The wrong assumption is that any line starting with ":word:" is a docinfo field.
- Root cause & location: module-level regex docinfo_re in sphinx/util/rst.py (docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')) is too permissive; prepend_prolog() uses it and therefore treats role/inline-role lines as docinfo, causing prolog insertion and extra blank lines that break title detection.
- Fix strategy: narrow docinfo_re so it only matches real docinfo field lines (require whitespace after the second colon, i.e. ":fieldname: value"). Change is local (one regex) and covers all places that depended on the old regex (there is one definition/use).
- Hardcoded values to change: docinfo_re value in sphinx/util/rst.py. Exact replacement: from ":\\w+:.*?" to r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s'.
- Methods to add/override: none required. RSTParser still calls prepend_prolog; only the regex changes.
- Investigation gaps: I did not inspect docutils' parser grammar source to 100% prove that "whitespace after the second colon" is the canonical docinfo delimiter (this is standard reST, but not proven here). Also I did not run an end-to-end parse of the reported minimal repro with the patched regex (recommend adding the unit test below).

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: docinfo_re is too permissive
What: In sphinx/util/rst.py the module variable docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') matches inline/domain roles like ":mod:`mypkg`". That makes prepend_prolog treat those lines as docinfo fields.  
Why it matters: prolog is inserted after those lines and a generated blank is added, breaking title detection for headings built from roles.  
Observed in: reading sphinx/util/rst.py (get_file_context).

### Finding 2: prepend_prolog behavior explains symptom
What: prepend_prolog(content, prolog) counts leading lines that match docinfo_re, inserts a blank after docinfo if any, then inserts the prolog at position pos and a trailing generated blank.  
Why it matters: if a role line is mis-classified as docinfo (pos > 0) the prolog ends up between role text and section underline (or creates a blank), preventing docutils from recognizing the section title.  
Location: sphinx/util/rst.py::prepend_prolog (get_file_context).

### Finding 3: only one code path needs changing
What: RSTParser.decorate (sphinx/parsers.py) calls prepend_prolog, but the only incorrect logic is the docinfo regex itself. No other sibling class or additional call-sites define/consume docinfo_re.  
Why it matters: change is localized to one regex definition; no breadth-of-change needed.  
Observed in: search for 'docinfo_re' and read of sphinx/parsers.py (search_codebase + get_file_context).

### Finding 4: i18n already avoids prolog in certain parses
What: sphinx/transforms/i18n.py temporarily clears config.rst_prolog when publishing msgstr, indicating some parsing paths already avoid applying rst_prolog.  
Why it matters: no additional changes needed for i18n-specific code paths.  
Observed in: reading sphinx/transforms/i18n.py (get_file_context).

### Finding 5: missing regression test for this exact case
What: existing tests cover prepend_prolog with proper docinfo (':title: ...') but there is no explicit test asserting that role syntax like ':mod:`x`' is NOT treated as docinfo.  
Why it matters: add a unit test to prevent regression.  
Observed in: tests/test_util_rst.py (get_file_context).

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: sphinx/util/rst.py:module.docinfo_re
- **Wrong assumption**: The code assumes any line that begins with ":<word>:" is a docinfo field (metadata).
- **Evidence**:
  - Current code: docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') (file sphinx/util/rst.py, line 28 as read).
  - prepend_prolog uses docinfo_re.match(line) to count leading docinfo lines and therefore treats ":mod:`mypkg`" as docinfo (role syntax matches ":mod:"), causing prolog insertion after the role line and insertion of '<generated>' blank lines (observed in prepend_prolog logic).
  - Tests in the repo show expected correct handling for lines like ':title: ...' (they have a space after the second colon) (tests/test_util_rst.py test_prepend_prolog).
- **Related locations**:
  - sphinx/util/rst.py::prepend_prolog uses docinfo_re.match(line) to count docinfo lines and perform insertion.
  - sphinx/parsers.RSTParser.decorate (sphinx/parsers.py) calls prepend_prolog — this is the call site that exhibits the symptom in practice.

Suggested exact replacement (one-line patch):
- Replace:
  - docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')
- With:
  - docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s')
  - Rationale: require whitespace after the second colon so ":field: value" matches but ":mod:`name`" (no space) does not.

### Location: sphinx/util/rst.py:prepend_prolog
- **Wrong assumption**: prepend_prolog assumes docinfo_re correctly identifies only metadata fields.
- **Evidence**:
  - Code path: prepend_prolog counts pos via docinfo_re.match(line); when pos>0 it inserts a generated blank (content.insert(pos, '', '<generated>', 0)) and then inserts prolog lines at pos—this behaviour is correct only if docinfo_re identifies only true docinfo fields.
  - With incorrect docinfo_re, these insertions occur in the middle of a role-based title and break section detection.
- **Related locations**:
  - sphinx/parsers.RSTParser.decorate calls prepend_prolog before docutils parsing; reporting and downstream (toctree/title extraction) failures originate here.

### Location: tests/test_util_rst.py:test_prepend_prolog (existing)
- **Wrong assumption tested previously**: tests assume docinfo detection must identify ':title:' and ':author:' (they contain a space after second colon). These tests pass both before and after the regex change because they conform to ":field: value" format.
- **Evidence**:
  - Existing assertion expects the prolog insertion after ':title: ...' lines (lines show '<rst_prolog>' items) — proves correct docinfo syntax includes whitespace and is preserved.
- **Related locations**:
  - Add a new test (see MID_LEVEL recommended snippet) to assert that ':mod:`name`' is not treated as docinfo.

Additional notes (investigation gaps):
- I did not inspect docutils C/Python source to strictly prove that docinfo fields always require whitespace after the colon; this is standard reST usage and all repo tests conform to it, but if there is an edge case where a docinfo field could legally have no whitespace, adjust the regex to allow a value-less field or use a more exact docutils field-list recognition. If desired, one could instead parse with docutils field-list rules, but that is heavier and unnecessary given standard reST conventions and existing tests.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each claim in the draft report against the repository source and the investigation summary. For each file or symbol I needed to inspect, I stated why I was fetching it and what I expected to learn; after each fetch I analyzed the result and incorporated exact evidence.

1) Verify the methods/files mentioned in the draft

- Reason to inspect sphinx/util/rst.py:
  - I expected to confirm the definition of docinfo_re and to read prepend_prolog to see how docinfo_re is used.

  What I fetched: sphinx/util/rst.py (lines 1–110).

  What I found:
  - The module-level regex definition is:
    - docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')  (line 28)
  - The prepend_prolog function uses that regex to count leading docinfo-like lines:
    - for line in content:
        if docinfo_re.match(line):
            pos += 1
        else:
            break
      (lines 81–86)
  - If pos > 0, prepend_prolog inserts a blank line with source '<generated>' at index pos and then inserts the prolog lines with source '<rst_prolog>', and finally inserts another '<generated>' blank. See inserts at lines 90–97:
    - content.insert(pos, '', '<generated>', 0)
    - content.insert(pos + lineno, line, '<rst_prolog>', lineno)
    - content.insert(pos + lineno + 1, '', '<generated>', 0)

  Analysis:
  - The code supports the draft claim that docinfo_re is used to detect leading docinfo lines and that prepend_prolog inserts blank/generated lines around inserted prolog content when docinfo lines are present.

- Reason to inspect sphinx/parsers.py:
  - I expected to confirm where prepend_prolog is invoked in the parsing flow.

  What I fetched: sphinx/parsers.py (lines 1–96).

  What I found:
  - RSTParser.decorate calls:
    - prepend_prolog(content, self.config.rst_prolog)
    - append_epilog(content, self.config.rst_epilog)
    (lines 83–86)

  Analysis:
  - This confirms that prepend_prolog is applied before the docutils state machine runs (decorate is called in parse before run), which means the misclassification at prepend_prolog would affect the docutils parsing stage and downstream title detection / toctree extraction.

- Reason to inspect tests/test_util_rst.py:
  - I expected to verify whether there is an existing test that would catch a docinfo regex that matches role-like lines and whether a negative test for role syntax exists.

  What I fetched: tests/test_util_rst.py (lines 1–109).

  What I found:
  - test_prepend_prolog checks behavior for leading lines like ':title: ...' and ':author: ...' (they include a space after the second colon).
  - There is no test that checks that a role/inline role such as ':mod:`name`' is NOT treated as docinfo.

  Analysis:
  - This confirms the draft claim that there is no regression test explicitly asserting that role syntax lines are not treated as docinfo.

- Reason to search for other occurrences of docinfo_re:
  - I expected to confirm that docinfo_re is defined/used only in sphinx/util/rst.py, i.e., the scope of the problem is localized.

  What I fetched: search for "docinfo_re" across the codebase.

  What I found:
  - Only occurrences are in sphinx/util/rst.py: the definition and its use in prepend_prolog.

  Analysis:
  - This supports the draft claim that the bug is localized to a single regex definition and its use in prepend_prolog.

- Reason to inspect sphinx/transforms/i18n.py (investigation noted a special-case):
  - I expected to confirm whether there are code paths that avoid applying rst_prolog (mitigations/alternate behavior).

  What I fetched: sphinx/transforms/i18n.py (excerpt lines 46–80).

  What I found:
  - publish_msgstr temporarily clears config.rst_prolog (lines 61–63 and restores it at the end, lines 78–79).
  - This shows that some parsing paths deliberately avoid applying rst_prolog.

  Analysis:
  - This supports the investigation note that certain code paths temporarily disable rst_prolog, which is relevant background but not an additional location of the defect itself.

2) Verify the RootCause: does the code support the stated root cause?

- The draft asserts the root cause is the overly permissive regex docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') in sphinx/util/rst.py and that this matches inline roles like ":mod:`name`".
  - Evidence: docinfo_re is defined exactly as docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') (sphinx/util/rst.py line 28).
  - The regex matches strings starting with ":" followed by word characters, then ":" and then any characters (including backticks). A leading role like ":mod:`mypkg`" starts with ":mod:" followed by a backtick, so docinfo_re.match(':mod:`mypkg`') will succeed. This supports the claim that role lines will be misclassified as docinfo.

- The draft asserts prepend_prolog inserts prolog and generated blank lines based on that detection, which breaks docutils' title detection for role-based titles.
  - Evidence: prepend_prolog iterates content and increments pos for each docinfo_re.match(line); if pos>0 it inserts a '<generated>' blank at pos and later inserts '<rst_prolog>' lines at pos and a trailing '<generated>' blank (sphinx/util/rst.py lines 78–97). Inserting a blank line between a title line and its following underline will prevent docutils from treating them as a section title. Therefore the code supports the chain described in the draft.

- The draft identifies RSTParser.decorate as the call site that applies prepend_prolog during parsing.
  - Evidence: RSTParser.decorate calls prepend_prolog(content, self.config.rst_prolog) (sphinx/parsers.py line 85). Also decorate is invoked by parse() before the state machine runs (sphinx/parsers.py lines 61–80). Therefore prepend_prolog is applied at parse-time and affects docutils parsing.

- The draft also claims tests do not include a negative case for role/faux-docinfo matching.
  - Evidence: tests/test_util_rst.py has tests exercising prepend_prolog with ':title:' and ':author:' but no test asserting that ':mod:`name`' should not be treated as docinfo. So the repo lacks a regression test for the exact failing case.

3) Check for claims in the draft not supported by code evidence

- The draft's statements are supported by the code I inspected. The only part where the draft goes beyond pure diagnosis is that it suggests a specific replacement regex docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s'). The repository does not contain that replacement; the suggested corrected relationship (requiring whitespace after the second colon) is a logical conclusion from the observed behavior and matches common reST docinfo syntax (":field: value"). The investigation noted that I did not check docutils grammar, so the claim that whitespace after the second colon is the canonical docinfo delimiter is reasonable but not exhaustively proven within this repository. This distinction should be noted: recommending a stricter regex is a diagnostic statement about the correct relationship rather than a prescribed code change, but I will present it as the correct relationship (not as a patch instruction) in RootCause.

4) Important findings from the investigation that the draft missed or should emphasize

- The investigation noted that publish_msgstr in sphinx/transforms/i18n.py temporarily clears rst_prolog during i18n message publishing; that is relevant contextual information showing not all parsing paths apply rst_prolog. The draft did mention only the main parse path; it did not mention this special-case behavior. This is context, not a separate defect location, but it matters for impact assessment (some parses already avoid prolog).

- The investigation stated there is only one definition/use of docinfo_re, which I verified. The draft includes this as "only one code path needs changing"; keep that.

5) Completeness check (other locations affected)

- I searched for uses of prepend_prolog and docinfo_re; found only:
  - docinfo_re and prepend_prolog in sphinx/util/rst.py
  - prepend_prolog called once in RSTParser.decorate (sphinx/parsers.py)
  - prepend_prolog covered by tests in tests/test_util_rst.py
- No other files define or use docinfo_re. Therefore the defect is localized: changing the docinfo detection heuristics in sphinx/util/rst.py affects all code paths that call prepend_prolog; in practice, the main call site is RSTParser.decorate and publish_msgstr disables rst_prolog explicitly in a particular i18n path.

6) Field discipline check — ensure each report field is focused correctly

- Description: should concisely describe the observed bug. The draft Description is appropriate: it states that when config.rst_prolog is set, documents whose top-level title is written using role/domain inline markup are not recognized as section titles.
- ObservedBehavior: must be user-visible symptoms only. The draft currently includes the insertion of blank/generated lines and mentions prolog insertion logic; that partially belongs in RootCause. bug_report.Title: Setting rst_prolog causes top-level headings that start with a role/domain (e.g. :mod:`...`) to be dropped or not included in toctree
bug_report.Description: When config.rst_prolog is set, documents whose top-level title is written using a role/domain inline markup (for example ":mod:`mypkg`") are not recognized as section titles and therefore do not render correctly or appear in the toctree. The issue occurs only when rst_prolog is present and the heading uses role/inline-role syntax as the document's first heading line.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Overly-permissive docinfo detection regex in sphinx/util/rst.py.
   - Location: sphinx/util/rst.py (module variable docinfo_re defined at line 28).
   - Problematic assumption: any line beginning with ":<word>:" is treated as a docinfo metadata field.
   - Code evidence: docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') (sphinx/util/rst.py:28). This pattern matches role/inline-role constructs like ":mod:`name`" because it only requires a colon, word characters, a colon, then any characters.
   - Correct relationship (diagnostic): docinfo fields in reST are metadata of the form ":field: value" (i.e. a second colon followed by whitespace and the field value). Docinfo detection should require that whitespace (or an explicit value delimiter) follows the second colon so role/inline-role forms (which typically have no whitespace after the second colon, e.g. ":mod:`name`") are not misclassified.

2) prepend_prolog assumes docinfo_re matches only metadata and uses that to choose where to insert the prolog.
   - Location: sphinx/util/rst.py::prepend_prolog (function beginning at line 78).
   - Problematic assumption: prepend_prolog treats any leading lines matched by docinfo_re as docinfo metadata and inserts a blank ('<generated>') after them and places the prolog after that position. Code evidence: prepend_prolog iterates over content and increments pos while docinfo_re.match(line) is true, then inserts generated blanks and prolog at that position (sphinx/util/rst.py:78–97).
   - Why this matters (diagnostic statement): if a role/inline-role line is misclassified as docinfo, the insertion of blank/prolog lines will separate the role line from the section underline (or otherwise alter the document structure), preventing docutils from recognizing the title.

3) The parse entry point applies prepend_prolog before docutils parsing, so misclassification affects title detection and toctree extraction.
   - Location: sphinx/parsers.py::RSTParser.decorate (line 83–86) which calls prepend_prolog(content, self.config.rst_prolog) prior to running the state machine.
   - Problematic assumption: calling prepend_prolog here is safe only if docinfo detection does not match role/inline-role lines. Because prepend_prolog is invoked at parse-time, any misclassification propagates into docutils parsing and downstream steps that rely on titles.

4) Lack of a regression test for role-lined titles.
   - Location: tests/test_util_rst.py::test_prepend_prolog (tests/test_util_rst.py lines 30–48).
   - Problematic assumption: existing tests verify prolog handling with conventional docinfo lines (e.g. ':title: ...' which include whitespace after the second colon) but do not include a negative test asserting that inline-role syntax such as ':mod:`x`' should not be treated as docinfo. This omission allowed the overly-permissive detection to go unchecked in unit tests.
StepsToReproduce: sphinx-quickstart --no-sep --project mypackage --author me -v 0.1.0 --release 0.1.0 --language en docs

def process_generate_options(app: Sphinx) -> None:
    genfiles = app.config.autosummary_generate

    if genfiles is True:
        env = app.builder.env
        genfiles = [env.doc2path(x, base=None) for x in env.found_docs
                    if os.path.isfile(env.doc2path(x))]
    elif genfiles is False:
        pass
    else:
        ext = list(app.config.source_suffix)
        genfiles = [genfile + (ext[0] if not genfile.endswith(tuple(ext)) else '')
                    for genfile in genfiles]

        for entry in genfiles[:]:
            if not path.isfile(path.join(app.srcdir, entry)):
                logger.warning(__('autosummary_generate: file not found: %s'), entry)
                genfiles.remove(entry)

    if not genfiles:
        return

    suffix = get_rst_suffix(app)
    if suffix is None:
        logger.warning(__('autosummary generats .rst files internally. '
                          'But your source_suffix does not contain .rst. Skipped.'))
        return

    from sphinx.ext.autosummary.generate import generate_autosummary_docs

    imported_members = app.config.autosummary_imported_members
    with mock(app.config.autosummary_mock_imports):
        generate_autosummary_docs(genfiles, suffix=suffix, base_path=app.srcdir,
                                  app=app, imported_members=imported_members,
                                  overwrite=app.config.autosummary_generate_overwrite)

def _underline(title: str, line: str = '=') -> str:
    if '\n' in title:

    return title + '\n' + line * len(title)

                                      system_templates_path)

        self.env.filters['escape'] = rst.escape
        self.env.filters['e'] = rst.escape

                self.env.install_gettext_translations(app.translator)  # type: ignore

        except TemplateNotFound:

    def render(self, template_name: str, context: Dict) -> str:
        """Render a template file."""
        try:
            template = self.env.get_template(template_name)
        except TemplateNotFound:
            try:

                template = self.env.get_template('autosummary/%s.rst' % template_name)
            except TemplateNotFound:

def generate_autosummary_content(name: str, obj: Any, parent: Any,
                                 template: AutosummaryRenderer, template_name: str,
                                 imported_members: bool, app: Any,
                                 recursive: bool, context: Dict) -> str:
    doc = get_documenter(app, obj, parent)

    def skip_member(obj: Any, name: str, objtype: str) -> bool:
        try:
            return app.emit_firstresult('autodoc-skip-member', objtype, name,
                                        obj, False, {})
        except Exception as exc:
            logger.warning(__('autosummary: failed to determine %r to be documented, '
                              'the following exception was raised:\n%s'),
                           name, exc, type='autosummary')
            return False

    def get_members(obj: Any, types: Set[str], include_public: List[str] = [],
                    imported: bool = True) -> Tuple[List[str], List[str]]:
        items = []  # type: List[str]
        public = []  # type: List[str]
        for name in dir(obj):
            try:
                value = safe_getattr(obj, name)
            except AttributeError:
                continue
            documenter = get_documenter(app, value, obj)
            if documenter.objtype in types:
                # skip imported members if expected
                if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__:
                    skipped = skip_member(value, name, documenter.objtype)
                    if skipped is True:
                        pass
                    elif skipped is False:
                        # show the member forcedly
                        items.append(name)
                        public.append(name)
                    else:
                        items.append(name)
                        if name in include_public or not name.startswith('_'):
                            # considers member as public
                            public.append(name)
        return public, items

    def get_module_attrs(members: Any) -> Tuple[List[str], List[str]]:
        """Find module attributes with docstrings."""
        attrs, public = [], []
        try:
            analyzer = ModuleAnalyzer.for_module(name)
            attr_docs = analyzer.find_attr_docs()
            for namespace, attr_name in attr_docs:
                if namespace == '' and attr_name in members:
                    attrs.append(attr_name)
                    if not attr_name.startswith('_'):
                        public.append(attr_name)
        except PycodeError:
            pass    # give up if ModuleAnalyzer fails to parse code
        return public, attrs

    def get_modules(obj: Any) -> Tuple[List[str], List[str]]:
        items = []  # type: List[str]
        for _, modname, ispkg in pkgutil.iter_modules(obj.__path__):
            fullname = name + '.' + modname
            items.append(fullname)
        public = [x for x in items if not x.split('.')[-1].startswith('_')]
        return public, items

    ns = {}  # type: Dict[str, Any]
    ns.update(context)

    if doc.objtype == 'module':
        ns['members'] = dir(obj)
        ns['functions'], ns['all_functions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'function'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['classes'], ns['all_classes'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'class'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['exceptions'], ns['all_exceptions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'exception'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = \
            get_module_attrs(ns['members'])
        ispackage = hasattr(obj, '__path__')
        if ispackage and recursive:
            ns['modules'], ns['all_modules'] = get_modules(obj)
    elif doc.objtype == 'class':
        ns['members'] = dir(obj)
        ns['inherited_members'] = \
            set(dir(obj)) - set(obj.__dict__.keys())
        ns['methods'], ns['all_methods'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'method'}, ['__init__'])
        ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'attribute', 'property'})

    modname, qualname = split_full_qualified_name(name)
    if doc.objtype in ('method', 'attribute', 'property'):
        ns['class'] = qualname.rsplit(".", 1)[0]

    if doc.objtype in ('class',):
        shortname = qualname
    else:
        shortname = qualname.rsplit(".", 1)[-1]

    ns['fullname'] = name
    ns['module'] = modname
    ns['objname'] = qualname
    ns['name'] = shortname

    ns['objtype'] = doc.objtype
    ns['underline'] = len(name) * '='

    if template_name:
        return template.render(template_name, ns)
    else:
        return template.render(doc.objtype, ns)


def generate_autosummary_docs(sources: List[str], output_dir: str = None,
                              suffix: str = '.rst', warn: Callable = None,
                              info: Callable = None, base_path: str = None,
                              builder: Builder = None, template_dir: str = None,
                              imported_members: bool = False, app: Any = None,
                              overwrite: bool = True) -> None:
    if info:
        warnings.warn('info argument for generate_autosummary_docs() is deprecated.',
                      RemovedInSphinx40Warning, stacklevel=2)
        _info = info
    else:
        _info = logger.info

    if warn:
        warnings.warn('warn argument for generate_autosummary_docs() is deprecated.',
                      RemovedInSphinx40Warning, stacklevel=2)
        _warn = warn
    else:
        _warn = logger.warning

    if builder:
        warnings.warn('builder argument for generate_autosummary_docs() is deprecated.',
                      RemovedInSphinx50Warning, stacklevel=2)

    if template_dir:
        warnings.warn('template_dir argument for generate_autosummary_docs() is deprecated.',
                      RemovedInSphinx50Warning, stacklevel=2)

    showed_sources = list(sorted(sources))
    if len(showed_sources) > 20:
        showed_sources = showed_sources[:10] + ['...'] + showed_sources[-10:]
    _info(__('[autosummary] generating autosummary for: %s') %
          ', '.join(showed_sources))

    if output_dir:
        _info(__('[autosummary] writing to %s') % output_dir)

    if base_path is not None:
        sources = [os.path.join(base_path, filename) for filename in sources]

    template = AutosummaryRenderer(app)

    # read
    items = find_autosummary_in_files(sources)

    # keep track of new files
    new_files = []

    # write
    for entry in sorted(set(items), key=str):
        if entry.path is None:
            # The corresponding autosummary:: directive did not have
            # a :toctree: option
            continue

        path = output_dir or os.path.abspath(entry.path)
        ensuredir(path)

        try:
            name, obj, parent, mod_name = import_by_name(entry.name)
        except ImportError as e:
            _warn(__('[autosummary] failed to import %r: %s') % (entry.name, e))
            continue

        context = {}
        if app:
            context.update(app.config.autosummary_context)

        content = generate_autosummary_content(name, obj, parent, template, entry.template,
                                               imported_members, app, entry.recursive, context)

        filename = os.path.join(path, name + suffix)
        if os.path.isfile(filename):
            with open(filename) as f:
                old_content = f.read()

            if content == old_content:
                continue
            elif overwrite:  # content has changed
                with open(filename, 'w') as f:
                    f.write(content)
                new_files.append(filename)
        else:
            with open(filename, 'w') as f:
                f.write(content)
            new_files.append(filename)

    # descend recursively to new files
    if new_files:
        generate_autosummary_docs(new_files, output_dir=output_dir,
                                  suffix=suffix, warn=warn, info=info,
                                  base_path=base_path,
                                  imported_members=imported_members, app=app,
                                  overwrite=overwrite)


# -- Finding documented entries in files ---------------------------------------

def find_autosummary_in_files(filenames: List[str]) -> List[AutosummaryEntry]:
    """Find out what items are documented in source/*.rst.

    See `find_autosummary_in_lines`.
    """
    documented = []  # type: List[AutosummaryEntry]
    for filename in filenames:
        with open(filename, encoding='utf-8', errors='ignore') as f:
            lines = f.read().splitlines()
            documented.extend(find_autosummary_in_lines(lines, filename=filename))

def test_mangle_signature():
    TEST = """
    () :: ()
    (a, b, c, d, e) :: (a, b, c, d, e)
    (a, b, c=1, d=2, e=3) :: (a, b[, c, d, e])
    (a, b, aaa=1, bbb=1, ccc=1, eee=1, fff=1, ggg=1, hhh=1, iii=1, jjj=1)\
    :: (a, b[, aaa, bbb, ccc, ...])
    (a, b, c=(), d=<foo>) :: (a, b[, c, d])
    (a, b, c='foobar()', d=123) :: (a, b[, c, d])
    (a, b[, c]) :: (a, b[, c])
    (a, b[, cxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx]) :: (a, b[, ...)
    (a, b='c=d, e=f, g=h', c=3) :: (a[, b, c])
    (a, b="c=d, e=f, g=h", c=3) :: (a[, b, c])
    (a, b='c=d, \\'e=f,\\' g=h', c=3) :: (a[, b, c])
    (a, b='c=d, ', e='\\\\' g=h, c=3) :: (a[, b, e, c])
    (a, b={'c=d, ': 3, '\\\\': 3}) :: (a[, b])
    (a=1, b=2, c=3) :: ([a, b, c])
    (a=1, b=<SomeClass: a, b, c>, c=3) :: ([a, b, c])
    (a=1, b=T(a=1, b=2), c=3) :: ([a, b, c])
    (a: int, b: int) -> str :: (a, b)
    """

    TEST = [[y.strip() for y in x.split("::")] for x in TEST.split("\n")
            if '::' in x]
    for inp, outp in TEST:
        res = mangle_signature(inp).strip().replace("\u00a0", " ")
        assert res == outp, ("'%s' -> '%s' != '%s'" % (inp, res, outp))


def test_extract_summary(capsys):
    settings = Mock(language_code='',
                    id_prefix='',
                    auto_id_prefix='',
                    pep_reference=False,
                    rfc_reference=False)
    document = new_document('', settings)

    # normal case
    doc = ['',
           'This is a first sentence. And second one.',
           '',
           'Second block is here']
    assert extract_summary(doc, document) == 'This is a first sentence.'

    # inliner case
    doc = ['This sentence contains *emphasis text having dots.*,',
           'it does not break sentence.']
    assert extract_summary(doc, document) == ' '.join(doc)

    # abbreviations
    doc = ['Blabla, i.e. bla.']
    assert extract_summary(doc, document) == 'Blabla, i.e.'

    # literal
    doc = ['blah blah::']
    assert extract_summary(doc, document) == 'blah blah.'

    # heading
    doc = ['blah blah',
           '=========']
    assert extract_summary(doc, document) == 'blah blah'

    _, err = capsys.readouterr()
    assert err == ''


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', **default_kw)
def test_get_items_summary(make_app, app_params):
    import sphinx.ext.autosummary
    import sphinx.ext.autosummary.generate
    args, kwargs = app_params
    app = make_app(*args, **kwargs)
    sphinx.ext.autosummary.generate.setup_documenters(app)
    # monkey-patch Autosummary.get_items so we can easily get access to it's
    # results..
    orig_get_items = sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items

    autosummary_items = {}

    def new_get_items(self, names, *args, **kwargs):
        results = orig_get_items(self, names, *args, **kwargs)
        for name, result in zip(names, results):
            autosummary_items[name] = result
        return results

    def handler(app, what, name, obj, options, lines):
        assert isinstance(lines, list)

        # ensure no docstring is processed twice:
        assert 'THIS HAS BEEN HANDLED' not in lines
        lines.append('THIS HAS BEEN HANDLED')
    app.connect('autodoc-process-docstring', handler)

    sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items = new_get_items
    try:
        app.builder.build_all()
    finally:
        sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items = orig_get_items

    html_warnings = app._warning.getvalue()
    assert html_warnings == ''

    expected_values = {
        'withSentence': 'I have a sentence which spans multiple lines.',
        'noSentence': "this doesn't start with a capital.",
        'emptyLine': "This is the real summary",
        'module_attr': 'This is a module attribute',
        'C.class_attr': 'This is a class attribute',
        'C.prop_attr1': 'This is a function docstring',
        'C.prop_attr2': 'This is a attribute docstring',
        'C.C2': 'This is a nested inner class docstring',
    }
    for key, expected in expected_values.items():
        assert autosummary_items[key][2] == expected, 'Summary for %s was %r -'\
            ' expected %r' % (key, autosummary_items[key], expected)

    # check an item in detail
    assert 'func' in autosummary_items
    func_attrs = ('func',
                  '(arg_, *args, **kwargs)',
                  'Test function take an argument ended with underscore.',
                  'dummy_module.func')
    assert autosummary_items['func'] == func_attrs


def str_content(elem):
    if elem.text is not None:
        return elem.text
    else:
        return ''.join(str_content(e) for e in elem)

def test_autosummary_generate(app, status, warning):
    app.builder.build_all()

    doctree = app.env.get_doctree('index')
    assert_node(doctree, (nodes.paragraph,
                          nodes.paragraph,
                          addnodes.tabular_col_spec,
                          autosummary_table,
                          [autosummary_toc, addnodes.toctree]))
    assert_node(doctree[3],
                [autosummary_table, nodes.table, nodes.tgroup, (nodes.colspec,
                                                                nodes.colspec,
                                                                [nodes.tbody, (nodes.row,
                                                                               nodes.row,
                                                                               nodes.row,
                                                                               nodes.row,
                                                                               nodes.row)])])
    assert_node(doctree[4][0], addnodes.toctree, caption="An autosummary")

    assert len(doctree[3][0][0][2]) == 5
    assert doctree[3][0][0][2][0].astext() == 'autosummary_dummy_module\n\n'
    assert doctree[3][0][0][2][1].astext() == 'autosummary_dummy_module.Foo()\n\n'
    assert doctree[3][0][0][2][2].astext() == 'autosummary_dummy_module.Foo.Bar\n\n'
    assert doctree[3][0][0][2][3].astext() == 'autosummary_dummy_module.bar(x[, y])\n\n'
    assert doctree[3][0][0][2][4].astext() == 'autosummary_dummy_module.qux\n\na module-level attribute'

    module = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'autosummary_dummy_module.rst').read_text()
    assert ('   .. autosummary::\n'
            '   \n'
            '      Foo\n'
            '   \n' in module)

    Foo = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'autosummary_dummy_module.Foo.rst').read_text()
    assert '.. automethod:: __init__' in Foo
    assert ('   .. autosummary::\n'
            '   \n'
            '      ~Foo.__init__\n'
            '      ~Foo.bar\n'
            '   \n' in Foo)
    assert ('   .. autosummary::\n'
            '   \n'
            '      ~Foo.baz\n'
            '   \n' in Foo)

    FooBar = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'autosummary_dummy_module.Foo.Bar.rst').read_text()
    assert ('.. currentmodule:: autosummary_dummy_module\n'
            '\n'
            '.. autoclass:: Foo.Bar\n' in FooBar)

    qux = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'autosummary_dummy_module.qux.rst').read_text()
    assert ('.. currentmodule:: autosummary_dummy_module\n'
            '\n'
            '.. autodata:: qux' in qux)

    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.module.rst').exists()
    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.module_importfail.rst').exists() is False
    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.package.rst').exists()
    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.package.module.rst').exists()

    # autosummary not having :recursive: option
    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package2.rst').exists()
    assert (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package2.module.rst').exists() is False

    # Check content of recursively generated stub-files
    content = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.rst').read_text()
    assert 'package.module' in content
    assert 'package.package' in content
    assert 'package.module_importfail' in content

    content = (app.srcdir / 'generated' / 'package.package.rst').read_text()
    assert 'package.package.module' in content

def test_import_by_name():
    import sphinx
    import sphinx.ext.autosummary

    prefixed_name, obj, parent, modname = import_by_name('sphinx')
    assert prefixed_name == 'sphinx'
    assert obj is sphinx
    assert parent is None
    assert modname == 'sphinx'

    prefixed_name, obj, parent, modname = import_by_name('sphinx.ext.autosummary.__name__')
    assert prefixed_name == 'sphinx.ext.autosummary.__name__'
    assert obj is sphinx.ext.autosummary.__name__
    assert parent is sphinx.ext.autosummary
    assert modname == 'sphinx.ext.autosummary'

    prefixed_name, obj, parent, modname = \
        import_by_name('sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items')
    assert prefixed_name == 'sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items'
    assert obj == sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary.get_items
    assert parent is sphinx.ext.autosummary.Autosummary
    assert modname == 'sphinx.ext.autosummary'

I'll try to view package templates at package_dir ext/autosummary/templates — but repository path likely has templates under 'sphinx/ext/autosummary/templates' but earlier attempt failed; maybe templates are inside package data not stored. However, generate_autosummary_content populates ns['members'] as dir(obj) unconditionally for modules when doc.objtype == 'module'. The bug report says 'members contains imported members even if autosummary_imported_members is False'. I see that imported_members flag is passed to generate_autosummary_content and used in get_members() for functions, classes, exceptions, but ns['members'] remains dir(obj) (which includes imported names). So the template variable 'members' includes everything. That seems to be the bug: ns['members'] should be filtered to respect imported_members. Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (summary)
- Change generate_autosummary_content so that ns['members'] honors the imported_members flag, i.e. when doc.objtype == 'module' and imported_members is False, filter dir(obj) to exclude imported members.
- Preserve behavior when imported_members is True (keep ns['members'] = dir(obj)).
- For filtering logic, follow the same idea used in get_members: include a name if getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__. But keep "special" dunder names (like __file__, __doc__, __name__, __package__, __spec__, __loader__, __cached__, __builtins__) so basic module metadata remains visible. Also continue to include attributes that don't have __module__ defined (these are usually simple values assigned in the module).

Concrete code change (patch)
- Replace (original code in generate.py around the ns building):

    if doc.objtype == 'module':
        ns['members'] = dir(obj)
        ns['functions'], ns['all_functions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'function'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['classes'], ns['all_classes'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'class'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['exceptions'], ns['all_exceptions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'exception'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = \
            get_module_attrs(ns['members'])
        ispackage = hasattr(obj, '__path__')
        if ispackage and recursive:
            ns['modules'], ns['all_modules'] = get_modules(obj)

- With this (proposed) replacement:

    if doc.objtype == 'module':
        if imported_members:
            members = list(dir(obj))
        else:
            members = []
            for name in dir(obj):
                try:
                    value = safe_getattr(obj, name)
                except AttributeError:
                    continue
                # Keep module metadata / dunder names (so basic info like
                # __file__, __name__, __doc__, etc. remain available in the template).
                if name.startswith('__') and name.endswith('__'):
                    members.append(name)
                    continue
                # Keep the attribute if it appears to be defined in this module
                # (same heuristic get_members uses for members: __module__ == module name).
                if getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__:
                    members.append(name)
        ns['members'] = members
        ns['functions'], ns['all_functions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'function'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['classes'], ns['all_classes'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'class'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['exceptions'], ns['all_exceptions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'exception'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = \
            get_module_attrs(ns['members'])
        ispackage = hasattr(obj, '__path__')
        if ispackage and recursive:
            ns['modules'], ns['all_modules'] = get_modules(obj)

Rationale for the code
- This uses the same test used elsewhere to decide if something is imported (value.__module__ vs module name), so it's consistent.
- It keeps dunder names so templates which rely on module metadata (e.g. __file__, __name__, __package__, __spec__) continue to see those.
- When imported_members True we preserve the previous behavior.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes that 'members' should be the raw output of dir(obj) (i.e. unfiltered) regardless of autosummary_imported_members; my fix changes the assumption to: when autosummary_imported_members is False, the 'members' list should contain only names that appear to be defined in the module (or the module metadata dunder names).

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- I examined generate.py for hardcoded numeric constants and such that relate to this assumption. The relevant hardcoded items are:
  - ns['members'] = dir(obj) (original unconditional assignment) — this encodes the assumption that templates always receive the full dir().
  - get_members uses the condition getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__ to detect imported vs local members; that is not a hardcoded constant but a heuristic that encodes the assumption that attributes defined in the module have __module__ == module name (true for functions, classes defined in the module). My fix relies on the same heuristic.
- Are any of these values still correct if the assumption is changed?
  - The unconditional use of dir(obj) is the line to remove/change (and I propose to change it).
  - The heuristic getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__ remains correct and is already used by get_members; it is the right test for functions/classes/objects that are bound to a module.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The "reverse" here would be any code that consumes ns['members'] expecting the old unfiltered list; this includes:
  - get_module_attrs(ns['members']) — which runs ModuleAnalyzer.find_attr_docs() and checks attr_name in members. Previously members was dir(obj) so any documented attributes found by ModuleAnalyzer would be in members if they were in dir(obj). With our change, members could be filtered and thus fewer attr_docs might be matched; but this is actually correct: if autosummary_imported_members is False, we want attrs only for real module attributes (not imported objects). ModuleAnalyzer returns attribute doc entries for attributes defined in source; those attributes should still be included because they will have names in dir(obj) and their values are module-defined so they should pass the getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__ check and remain in members. The only module attributes that might be lost are ones that have no value or weird cases; but previously they also would have been in dir(obj) and ModuleAnalyzer might have found them — however a documented attribute should correspond to an entry in dir(obj) and have a value; if the attribute is a simple constant (like an int), getattr(value, '__module__', None) may be None; my proposed code includes such names because getattr(value, '__module__', None) will be None, not equal to obj.__name__, so they'd be excluded. Wait — that's a corner: variables assigned a simple int or string in the module do not have __module__ attribute; getattr(..., None) is None; so they would be excluded by the strict test. But get_members only covers functions/classes/exceptions; variables with docstrings are found by ModuleAnalyzer.find_attr_docs and require that attr_name in members — if our filtering excludes such attr_name because the value has no __module__, then get_module_attrs will not find them. We need to ensure that module attributes (e.g. constants) remain included even if value has no __module__.
- Therefore adjust the rule: include names if either:
    - name is dunder (keep module metadata), OR
    - getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__, OR
    - value has no __module__ attribute (getattr returns None) BUT the attribute is present in the module's own dictionary (obj.__dict__) or otherwise obvious to be local.

  We need to be practical because obj might be a module object; checking obj.__dict__ membership is robust: if name in getattr(obj, '__dict__', {}), that means the attribute is defined on the module object (not imported by attribute access from elsewhere). So using name in getattr(obj, '__dict__', {}) is the safest way to detect module-defined attributes. That suggests modifying the logic to:

      if name in getattr(obj, '__dict__', {}):
          members.append(name)
      elif getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__:
          members.append(name)
      elif name is dunder: members.append(name)

  This handles constants (which are in obj.__dict__) and functions/classes (which are also in __dict__ and also have __module__). It will also exclude imported module attributes like os (os is in obj.__dict__ only if the module assigned it at top-level — but that assignment would put 'os' into the module's __dict__ indeed; so name in obj.__dict__ would be True for os). Wait — that's important: when a module does "import os", name 'os' is created in obj.__dict__ by the import statement, so name in obj.__dict__ is True even for imports. That would make the rule include imported names, defeating the point.

  So name in obj.__dict__ cannot be used to detect imported vs local: imports are assignments in module's source, so imported objects are present in the module's __dict__ just like local constants. So we need a different detection: we should check the object's own __module__ to see where it was defined; for simple constants (int, str) value.__module__ is usually 'builtins' and not equal to module name — but these values are not imported members in the sense of "module imports should be omitted" (the user's example only excluded the imported module os). The user likely expects that imported objects that are modules (like os) or functions/classes from other modules should be omitted, but simple constants (e.g. FOO = 1) should remain. That suggests the earlier heuristic (value.__module__ == obj.__name__) is good for functions/classes, but simple constants won't pass that test. Yet in the user's example, the dunder names were expected to remain visible — and simple constants would probably be desired to remain.

  So, to preserve attributes like constants and module metadata, but exclude imported members that are modules or whose __module__ is not the module, I'll propose the following test:

    - Keep dunder names.
    - For values that are module objects: include only if value.__name__ == obj.__name__ (same module).
    - For values that have a __module__ attribute (callable types, classes, functions, etc.): include only if value.__module__ == obj.__name__.
    - For values that are plain builtins/immutable types (int, str, etc.) which won't have __module__ meaningful to the user, include them (they are module-level variables, not imports in the sense of "import module" we want to hide). How can we distinguish between a module-level variable assigned by a literal constant vs an import? A direct way is to attempt to inspect the source with ModuleAnalyzer to see if name is assigned via an import statement — but that's overkill and fragile.

  However, simpler and reasonable approach used by many tools: exclude names whose value is a module and whose module name != obj.__name__, and exclude values whose __module__ != obj.__name__ and which are not simple builtins (i.e. if value has a __module__ attribute at all, require it to match obj.__name__). For simple constants like int and str, getattr(value, '__module__', None) returns 'builtins' and would not equal obj.__name__, so they'd be excluded incorrectly by this rule. But get_members (which controls things like functions and classes) doesn't attempt to include constants. Meanwhile get_module_attrs is supposed to find module attributes with docstrings — these attributes are discovered by ModuleAnalyzer and their names are compared against members passed in. For constants with attribute docstrings (i.e., attribute docstrings found by ModuleAnalyzer), they are probably documented via special code and are probably objects with docstrings attached — their values would likely be something like names created by assignments to objects with docstrings; unusual but possible.

  To avoid over-complicating, the minimal practical fix that matches the user's example and intended behavior is:
    - Exclude names whose value is a module object and whose module name != obj.__name__ (this handles 'os' correctly).
    - Exclude names whose value has a __module__ attribute and where value.__module__ != obj.__name__ and value is not a built-in simple type — but testing for "simple types" is error-prone.
    - Keep dunder names.

  For a robust and conservative fix, do:
    - Keep dunder names (so module metadata is present).
    - For each name, fetch value via safe_getattr. If it is an instance of ModuleType and value.__name__ != obj.__name__, skip (this filters imported modules like os).
    - If it has a __module__ attribute and value.__module__ != obj.__name__, skip (this filters imported functions/classes).
    - Otherwise, include. This will include constants and other values even if value.__module__ == 'builtins' (which is acceptable — those are not "imported module members" in the sense of importing from another module). The user example excludes only os; this rule excludes os and imported functions/classes.

  That is the policy I will adopt in the proposed patch.

  Re-check get_module_attrs: it checks attr_name in members. For module-level attributes (constants) that should still appear in members under this policy. For attributes documented by the analyzer because they have attribute docstrings, those are likely present as entries in dir(obj) and either be constants (kept) or objects defined in module (kept). So get_module_attrs should still work.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods / functions in generate.py:
  - get_members still works unchanged; it continues to honor imported argument and the __module__ test.
  - get_module_attrs uses the members list passed. Under the new rules it will still find documented attributes because attribute names documented in source are usually not imported modules. If a documented attribute is an imported object (rare) then if autosummary_imported_members is False it should be filtered out — consistent behavior.
  - generate_autosummary_docs' recursive generation, file writing, and reading logic are unaffected.
  - The autosummary extension process_generate_options still passes the config value unchanged. No other functions depend on ns['members'] except templates and get_module_attrs; user templates that expected the raw dir() will see the new filtered list when autosummary_imported_members is False. That is the desired behavior.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new helper methods are strictly required. We will reuse safe_getattr and existing ModuleAnalyzer helpers. generate_autosummary_content will need a bit more conditional code as shown above. No parent methods or overrides are required (there is no class to override in this context).

Edge cases & test implications
- Tests that assumed ns['members'] contained imported modules will need to be updated — but this change matches the config semantics (autosummary_imported_members False should hide imported members).
- A more fine-grained heuristic may be desired in edge cases (e.g., a module that intentionally assigns imported objects to names — but by definition they are imported). The change is consistent with the flag semantics.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis + locations to change + patch)

Root cause
- In generate_autosummary_content() (sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py), the template context key 'members' is set to dir(obj) unconditionally (ns['members'] = dir(obj)). This ignores the imported_members flag passed into generate_autosummary_content and causes templates that iterate 'members' to see imported members (like os) even when autosummary_imported_members is False. Other parts of the function (get_members called for functions/classes/exceptions) correctly incorporate the imported_members flag; only ns['members'] was left unfiltered.

Every location that needs to change
1) sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py
- Current behavior:
  - When generating a module template context, the code sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) unconditionally (lines ~248–249).
- Problem/assumption encoded:
  - Assumes templates always want the entire dir() listing regardless of autosummary_imported_members config.
- The fix:
  - Replace the unconditional assignment with logic that honors imported_members:
    - If imported_members is True, keep ns['members'] = dir(obj).
    - If imported_members is False, set ns['members'] to a filtered list excluding imported members, using the same heuristics as get_members for functions/classes (i.e. skip values where value.__module__ != obj.__name__), and also excluding imported module objects (ModuleType whose __name__ != module name). Keep dunder names so module metadata remains available in templates.

Patch (conceptual snippet to apply at the location where ns is built in generate_autosummary_content):

    if doc.objtype == 'module':
        if imported_members:
            members = list(dir(obj))
        else:
            members = []
            for name in dir(obj):
                try:
                    value = safe_getattr(obj, name)
                except AttributeError:
                    continue
                # keep module metadata / dunder names
                if name.startswith('__') and name.endswith('__'):
                    members.append(name)
                    continue
                # skip module objects coming from other modules (e.g. imported modules)
                if isinstance(value, ModuleType):
                    if getattr(value, '__name__', None) != obj.__name__:
                        continue
                    else:
                        members.append(name)
                        continue
                # for callables/classes/etc., skip if they were defined in another module
                vmod = getattr(value, '__module__', None)
                if vmod is not None:
                    if vmod != obj.__name__:
                        continue
                # otherwise include the name (constants, values, etc.)
                members.append(name)

        ns['members'] = members
        ns['functions'], ns['all_functions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'function'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['classes'], ns['all_classes'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'class'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['exceptions'], ns['all_exceptions'] = \
            get_members(obj, {'exception'}, imported=imported_members)
        ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = \
            get_module_attrs(ns['members'])
        ispackage = hasattr(obj, '__path__')
        if ispackage and recursive:
            ns['modules'], ns['all_modules'] = get_modules(obj)

Notes about the above:
- The code uses safe_getattr (already imported) and ModuleType (module) — ModuleType is already imported at top of sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py, but not in generate.py. generate.py currently imports pkgutil, pydoc, etc. The fix needs to import ModuleType at top of generate.py:
    from types import ModuleType
  (Add this import near other imports in generate.py.)

Why this addresses the bug
- This change ensures the 'members' variable presented to templates excludes imported modules and members whose __module__ differs from the module being documented when autosummary_imported_members is False, aligning ns['members'] with how get_members filters functions/classes/exceptions and with the developer's intended meaning of the config option.

Any other locations to change
- No other code needs change for this bug:
  - process_generate_options in sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py already reads autosummary_imported_members and passes it into generate_autosummary_docs; no change required there.
  - get_members already respects the imported flag and requires no change.
  - Templates shipped with autosummary that iterate 'members' will now receive filtered member lists when autosummary_imported_members is False. If there are user templates that rely on seeing imported modules in members, they will have to set autosummary_imported_members = True in conf.py when generating those stubs.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None beyond the change to how ns['members'] is created. There were no numeric constants or similar that encode the old assumption. The heuristic relies on value.__module__ which is already used in get_members.

Tests to check / update
- There is already a test in tests/test_ext_autosummary.py for imported members (search result showed a test). Ensure the test expects that members in the generated module template do not include imported modules if autosummary_imported_members is False. If the test only checked get_members behavior (functions/classes) it may not cover members variable; add or update a test that checks the generated module.rst template contains or does not contain specific imported names based on config.

Summary (concise)
- Root cause: ns['members'] for module templates was set to dir(obj) unconditionally in generate_autosummary_content, ignoring autosummary_imported_members.
- Fix: change generate_autosummary_content to set ns['members'] to a filtered list when imported_members is False, excluding imported module objects and objects whose __module__ != module name, while keeping module metadata (dunder names) and simple module-level values.
- Files to change:
  - sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py: modify generate_autosummary_content as shown, and add "from types import ModuleType" to imports.
- No other code changes required.

If you want, I can:
- produce an exact git-style patch/diff for the change (including import addition),
- or run through likely unit-test additions/changes (example test cases to add to tests/test_ext_autosummary.py) that assert generated module templates no longer include imported modules when config.autosummary_imported_members is False.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: templates get ns['members'] containing imported names (e.g. "os") even when autosummary_imported_members is False — the code assumes members == dir(obj) unconditionally.
- Root cause: generate_autosummary_content() in sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) without honoring the imported_members flag; other helpers (get_members) do honor the flag.
- Fix strategy: in generate_autosummary_content(), when doc.objtype == 'module' and imported_members is False, replace the unconditional ns['members'] = dir(obj) with filtered logic that excludes imported modules/objects (use same heuristic as get_members: check value.__module__ and ModuleType). Preserve dunder metadata names.
- Hardcoded values to change: the unconditional use of dir(obj) for ns['members'] must be made conditional; no numeric constants, but add import from types import ModuleType in generate.py.
- New methods: none required — reuse safe_getattr and existing get_members/get_module_attrs. Update only generate_autosummary_content.
- Gaps in investigation: did not inspect packaged Jinja templates shipped with autosummary (couldn't read templates path), and did not exhaustively test edge cases for simple constants and ModuleAnalyzer attribute discovery — these should be validated in tests.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: ns['members'] is populated unconditionally (root cause) (Checkpoint 1 & 2)
What: In sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py, generate_autosummary_content() sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) when doc.objtype == 'module'. Why it matters: this ignores the imported_members argument/config and is the direct cause of imported names appearing in module templates.

### Finding 2: get_members already respects autosummary_imported_members (Checkpoint 2 & 3)
What: get_members(obj, types, imported=imported_members) uses the test "if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__" to skip imported objects. Why it matters: shows the intended filtering heuristic and proves consistency — ns['members'] should use the same heuristic.

### Finding 3: config value is wired into generator (Checkpoint 1)
What: sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and passes it into generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=...). Why it matters: the generator sees the config and must act on it — currently it does for get_members but not for ns['members'].

### Finding 4: Proposed filtering rule and required import (Checkpoint 4)
What: Filter dir(obj) when imported_members is False by skipping:
  - module objects (types.ModuleType) whose __name__ != module.__name__
  - objects with a __module__ attribute where value.__module__ != module.__name__
  Keep dunder names. Why it matters: aligns ns['members'] with get_members behavior and matches user expectation (hides 'os'). Also requires adding "from types import ModuleType" to generate.py.

### Finding 5: Edge-case gap — simple constants & ModuleAnalyzer (Checkpoint 4c)
What: naive filtering by value.__module__ might inadvertently exclude simple module-level constants or attributes discovered by ModuleAnalyzer. Why it matters: get_module_attrs(ns['members']) uses ns['members'] to match attribute docs; ensure constants and documented attributes remain visible. This is an uncovered path that needs unit-test verification.

### Finding 6: Tests to review (Checkpoint 1 & 4)
What: tests/test_ext_autosummary.py contains autosummary imported-members tests; they must be checked/expanded to assert that the module template variable members no longer contains imported modules when autosummary_imported_members is False. Why it matters: to prevent regressions.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_content
- **Wrong assumption**: ns['members'] should always be the raw result of dir(obj) (i.e., ns['members'] = dir(obj)).
- **Evidence**: generate_autosummary_content() contains the exact line:
    ns['members'] = dir(obj)
  (observed while reading sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py; this assignment occurs unconditionally when doc.objtype == 'module'.)
- **Related locations**:
  - sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_members — uses imported flag and the condition "if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__" (this is the heuristic to reuse).
  - sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_module_attrs — consumes ns['members'] to match attribute docs; it depends on members containing the intended names.

### Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py::process_generate_options (or caller)
- **Wrong assumption**: none in code, but evidence that config is passed through and expected to affect generation.
- **Evidence**: process_generate_options reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and calls generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=app.config.autosummary_imported_members) (search result and file context show this wiring).
- **Related locations**:
  - sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_docs — receives imported_members and passes it to generate_autosummary_content.

### Location: tests/test_ext_autosummary.py (test file noted by search)
- **Wrong assumption**: tests may only assert get_members filtering and not check ns['members'] behavior.
- **Evidence**: repository contains tests/test_ext_autosummary.py with tests referencing imported-members behavior; these should be examined/extended to cover module template ns['members'].
- **Related locations**:
  - The new behavior must be validated against existing tests to prevent regressions.

Notes / exact changes to apply (evidence)
- Add to top of sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py:
    from types import ModuleType
- Replace the block where ns['members'] is set (currently "ns['members'] = dir(obj)") with conditional filtering using safe_getattr + ModuleType + the __module__ heuristic. Example filtering logic (exact variable names used above):
    if imported_members:
        members = list(dir(obj))
    else:
        members = []
        for name in dir(obj):
            try:
                value = safe_getattr(obj, name)
            except AttributeError:
                continue
            if name.startswith('__') and name.endswith('__'):
                members.append(name)
                continue
            if isinstance(value, ModuleType):
                if getattr(value, '__name__', None) != obj.__name__:
                    continue
                else:
                    members.append(name)
                    continue
            vmod = getattr(value, '__module__', None)
            if vmod is not None and vmod != obj.__name__:
                continue
            members.append(name)
    ns['members'] = members

- Rationale: This uses the same __module__ check as get_members and explicitly filters imported module objects (e.g. "os"), while preserving module metadata dunder names and simple constants.
