Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify the draft report's claims against the repository source. I will (a) locate and read the functions named in the draft, (b) check how the autosummary_imported_members config is read and passed, (c) confirm how template variable ns['members'] is produced and consumed, and (d) check test coverage references the draft mentions.

1) Why I read sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py
- I expect to confirm whether generate_autosummary_content sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) unconditionally for modules and classes, and to inspect get_members() and get_module_attrs() implementations to compare their member-filtering logic.

Result and analysis:
- I opened sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py and inspected generate_autosummary_content and related helpers.
  - generate_autosummary_content: when doc.objtype == 'module' it does:
    - ns['members'] = dir(obj)  (line 249 in the file content I read)
    - ns['functions'], ns['all_functions'] = get_members(obj, {'function'}, imported=imported_members) (lines 250-251)
    - ns['classes'], ns['all_classes'] = get_members(obj, {'class'}, imported=imported_members) (lines 252-253)
    - ns['exceptions'], ns['all_exceptions'] = get_members(obj, {'exception'}, imported=imported_members) (lines 254-255)
    - ns['attributes'], ns['all_attributes'] = get_module_attrs(ns['members']) (lines 256-257)
  - For classes, generate_autosummary_content sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) as well (line 262).
  - get_members: defined inside generate_autosummary_content (lines 195-220) iterates over dir(obj) but uses safe_getattr() and get_documenter() and applies this test before including a member:
      if documenter.objtype in types:
          if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__:
             ... append name ...
    (the key condition is at lines 205-208).
  - get_module_attrs: defined to use ModuleAnalyzer.for_module(name) and attr_docs; it matches attr_name in members (lines 222-235; note it checks "if namespace == '' and attr_name in members:").

Conclusion from generate.py:
- The file contains the exact unconditional assignment ns['members'] = dir(obj) for modules and classes.
- The helper get_members respects the imported flag (via the "imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__" test) but ns['members'] is assigned unconditionally from dir(obj) and is not filtered by imported_members.
- get_module_attrs depends on the value of ns['members'] to decide which module attributes have docs.

2) Why I read sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py
- I expect to confirm whether the autosummary_imported_members config is read and passed into the generate functions (i.e., to confirm wiring the config into generation).

Result and analysis:
- In process_generate_options (lines ~722-756) the code reads:
    imported_members = app.config.autosummary_imported_members  (line 752)
  and then calls generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=imported_members) (line 754-756).
- generate_autosummary_docs then calls generate_autosummary_content(..., imported_members, ...) (generate_autosummary_docs line 361 shows the call).

Conclusion:
- The config value app.config.autosummary_imported_members is read in __init__.py and passed as imported_members to generate_autosummary_docs/generate_autosummary_content. However, generate_autosummary_content does not use imported_members when setting ns['members'] for modules (it uses dir(obj) unconditionally).

3) Why I inspected tests/test_ext_autosummary.py and the test root for imported_members
- I expect to check whether tests assert the behavior of template variable members and whether tests exercise autosummary_imported_members set to False.

Result and analysis:
- tests/test_ext_autosummary.py contains a test test_autosummary_imported_members (line ~349 onward). That test uses the test root test-ext-autosummary-imported_members which has its conf.py set autosummary_imported_members = True (tests/roots/test-ext-autosummary-imported_members/conf.py lines 5-7).
- The test asserts that certain member names (Bar and foo) are present when autosummary_imported_members is True (lines 356-364).
- I did not find a test asserting the behavior when autosummary_imported_members is False, nor a test asserting absence of imported module names in the generated module template when autosummary_imported_members is False.

Conclusion:
- Tests cover the True case but not the False case for module template ns['members'] semantics; the draft's claim that tests do not currently assert module template ns['members'] semantics is supported.

4) Verify every draft claim against code and identify unsupported claims or places needing softening
- Claim: "Unconditional population of ns['members'] from dir(obj) in generate_autosummary_content." — Supported. Code: ns['members'] = dir(obj) (generate.py line 249).
- Claim: "The autosummary configuration value is read and passed into the generator but not honored for module template members." — Supported. Code: __init__.py reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and passes it (line 752) and generate_autosummary_content uses imported_members for get_members but not for ns['members'].
- Claim: "get_members already respects the imported flag using the condition if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__" — Supported; see get_members (lines 205-208).
- Claim: "Module-object handling and dunder preservation are required for correct filtering; skip attributes when they are ModuleType whose __name__ != module.__name__, and skip attributes whose getattr(value, '__module__', None) != module.__name__. Preserve dunder names." — Partially supported / needs softening:
    - The repository's current get_members does not explicitly check for ModuleType; instead it relies on get_documenter(obj, value, obj) to classify the attribute and then only consider attributes whose documenter.objtype is in the desired types. That means module-type attributes (e.g. imported module 'os') are normally not included by get_members when types are {'function','class','exception'}. So it is correct to say module objects are effectively excluded by the get_members path; but the claim that the correct filtering rule must include an explicit ModuleType check is prescriptive. The investigation observed that directory-based ns['members'] should be filtered to match the behavior of get_members, and that excluding imported modules like 'os' is required. I will rephrase RootCause to describe the mismatch and the intended membership relationship (the exact condition get_members uses).
- Claim: "ns['members'] is consumed by get_module_attrs and other routines; incorrect ns['members'] causes downstream mismatches" — Supported. generate_autosummary_content passes ns['members'] into get_module_attrs (lines 256-257) which uses it to select attr_docs (lines 226-235).
- Claim: "Tests do not currently assert the module template ns['members'] semantics" — Supported (the test root for imported_members sets it True; no test for False was found).

5) Completeness / impact check: other locations affected by the same pattern
- generate_autosummary_content sets ns['members'] = dir(obj) for both modules and classes (module at line 249; class at line 262). Both assignments are unconditional uses of dir(obj). The root cause pattern (unfiltered members set from dir(obj)) therefore exists in two places; both must be mentioned.
- ns['members'] is used by get_module_attrs (lines 222-235) to decide which module attributes have docs; changing ns['members'] affects attribute-doc matching.
- get_members is used for functions/classes/exceptions (lines 250-255) and already respects imported_members. The mismatch is specifically between ns['members'] and the filtered lists that get_members produces.
- No other file in the codebase sets the template variable 'members' elsewhere; search for "ns['members']" showed only generate.py locations.

6) Field discipline and content rework plan
- Description should state the observed problem concisely (the module template variable members contains imported names when autosummary_imported_members is False).
- RootCause should list the exact incorrect assumptions and the precise code locations, and state the intended/observed formulas or relationships (i.e., get_members's condition), but must not prescribe fixes.
- StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior should be left as user-visible behaviors only.

7) Unsupported or overstated claims in the draft
- The draft’s RootCause contained specific code-snippet style "proposed filtering logic" and explicit "add from types import ModuleType" — that is a repair prescription. The guidelines forbid prescribing fixes. I will remove/soften any prescriptive code suggestions from RootCause and only report the diagnostic facts: what code does, where, and what relationship it should have used (quoting the condition used by get_members).
- The draft states "Wrong assumption: generate_autosummary_content assumes no special ModuleType or __module__ checks are necessary when building ns['members']." The code shows generate_autosummary_content indeed assigns dir(obj) unconditionally; I will rephrase to say that generate_autosummary_content does not apply imported_members filtering, whereas get_members does.

8) Ensure all secondary findings from the investigation are included with equal weight
- The investigation listed multiple related findings. I verified the following and will include each with equal weight in the RootCause:
  - Unconditional ns['members'] = dir(obj) for modules.
  - generate_autosummary_content sets ns['members'] for classes unconditionally as well (same pattern).
  - get_members applies imported-members filtering (the condition), so ns['members'] is inconsistent with the filtered lists.
  - get_module_attrs uses ns['members'] to select documented attributes, so an unfiltered members list affects attribute detection.
  - process_generate_options reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and passes it to generation; thus the config is wired but not honored for ns['members'].
  - Tests cover the True case but not False for module template members (coverage gap).

Phase 1 conclusion
- The draft report's central diagnostic claims are supported by the code:
  - generate_autosummary_content assigns ns['members'] = dir(obj) unconditionally for modules (generate.py line ~249) and classes (line ~262).
  - get_members (generate.py lines ~195-220) does apply filtering using the imported flag and "getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__".
  - The config autosummary_imported_members is read and passed (autosummary/__init__.py lines ~750-756).
  - get_module_attrs depends on ns['members'] to match attribute docs (generate.py lines ~222-235 and 256-257).
- The draft included prescriptive code-change suggestions (explicit code replacement and ModuleType import). Per the instructions, I will not include these in the revised report; I will rephrase RootCause strictly as diagnosis and include the same set of findings the investigation produced, giving equal weight to each.

bug_report.Title: autosummary: module template 'members' includes imported members despite autosummary_imported_members=False
bug_report.Description: When generating autosummary module pages, the template variable `members` contains imported names (for example, the imported module 'os') even when conf.py sets autosummary_imported_members = False. As a result, generated module autosummary blocks list imported modules/objects in the template variable `members`.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) generate_autosummary_content unconditionally assigns template variable members from dir(obj) for modules and classes.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_content (module case: ns['members'] = dir(obj); class case: ns['members'] = dir(obj]). The code sets ns['members'] directly from dir(obj) without applying the imported_members configuration.

2) The autosummary imported-members configuration is read and passed into the generator but not applied to the members variable.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py::process_generate_options reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and calls generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=...). That imported_members argument is forwarded into generate_autosummary_content, but generate_autosummary_content does not use it when populating ns['members'].

3) Helper functions apply a different (filtered) membership heuristic than ns['members'].
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_members. get_members uses the imported flag and the condition
         if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__
     to decide whether to include a candidate member. This shows the intended membership relationship when imported_members is False; however, ns['members'] (set from dir(obj)) does not reflect this filtered view.

4) Module attribute detection depends on the unfiltered members list and therefore is affected by the discrepancy.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_module_attrs consumes the ns['members'] value to select documented module attributes (ModuleAnalyzer.find_attr_docs results are matched against members). An unfiltered ns['members'] can therefore change which attributes are considered documented or appear in templates.

5) The unconditional dir(obj) population pattern appears in both module and class branches of generate_autosummary_content.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py (module branch lines setting ns['members'] = dir(obj); class branch also sets ns['members'] = dir(obj)), indicating the same root-cause pattern in multiple locations.

6) Existing tests exercise the imported-members behavior in some cases but do not assert the module-template ns['members'] semantics for the False case.
   - Location: tests/test_ext_autosummary.py::test_autosummary_imported_members and tests/roots/test-ext-autosummary-imported_members/conf.py (the testroot sets autosummary_imported_members = True). There is no test asserting that the generated module template variable members excludes imported modules when autosummary_imported_members is False.
StepsToReproduce: Create _templates/autosummary/module.rst with a module autosummary template that iterates over members and prints each item.
Create example.py containing: import os
Create index.rst that references example in an autosummary toctree.
In conf.py set autosummary_generate = True and autosummary_imported_members = False.

Build the documentation with Sphinx (e.g. make html or sphinx-build) and inspect generated/example.rst; the generated module autosummary will list imported members such as 'os' in the autosummary block.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When autosummary_imported_members is False, the template variable `members` exposed to module templates should not include names that come from imported modules/objects (for example, 'os'). The generated module autosummary should reflect only the module's own attributes (and module metadata names as appropriate), not imported modules.

"""
    test_napoleon_docstring
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

    Tests for :mod:`sphinx.ext.napoleon.docstring` module.


    :copyright: Copyright 2007-2020 by the Sphinx team, see AUTHORS.
    :license: BSD, see LICENSE for details.
"""

from collections import namedtuple
from inspect import cleandoc
from textwrap import dedent
from unittest import TestCase, mock

from sphinx.ext.napoleon import Config
from sphinx.ext.napoleon.docstring import GoogleDocstring, NumpyDocstring


class NamedtupleSubclass(namedtuple('NamedtupleSubclass', ('attr1', 'attr2'))):
    """Sample namedtuple subclass

    Attributes
    ----------
    attr1 : Arbitrary type
        Quick description of attr1
    attr2 : Another arbitrary type
        Quick description of attr2
    attr3 : Type

        Adds a newline after the type

    """
    # To avoid creating a dict, as a namedtuple doesn't have it:
    __slots__ = ()

    def __new__(cls, attr1, attr2=None):
        return super().__new__(cls, attr1, attr2)


class BaseDocstringTest(TestCase):
    pass


class NamedtupleSubclassTest(BaseDocstringTest):
    def test_attributes_docstring(self):
        config = Config()
        actual = str(NumpyDocstring(cleandoc(NamedtupleSubclass.__doc__),
                     config=config, app=None, what='class',
                     name='NamedtupleSubclass', obj=NamedtupleSubclass))
        expected = """\
Sample namedtuple subclass

.. attribute:: attr1
   :type: Arbitrary type

   Quick description of attr1

.. attribute:: attr2
   :type: Another arbitrary type

   Quick description of attr2

.. attribute:: attr3
   :type: Type

   Adds a newline after the type
"""

        self.assertEqual(expected, actual)


class InlineAttributeTest(BaseDocstringTest):

    def test_class_data_member(self):
        config = Config()
        docstring = dedent("""\
        data member description:

        - a: b
        """)
        actual = str(GoogleDocstring(docstring, config=config, app=None,
                     what='attribute', name='some_data', obj=0))
        expected = dedent("""\
        data member description:

        - a: b""")

        self.assertEqual(expected, actual)

    def test_class_data_member_inline(self):
        config = Config()
        docstring = """b: data member description with :ref:`reference`"""
        actual = str(GoogleDocstring(docstring, config=config, app=None,
                     what='attribute', name='some_data', obj=0))
        expected = dedent("""\
        data member description with :ref:`reference`

        :type: b""")
        self.assertEqual(expected, actual)

    def test_class_data_member_inline_no_type(self):
        config = Config()
        docstring = """data with ``a : in code`` and :ref:`reference` and no type"""
        actual = str(GoogleDocstring(docstring, config=config, app=None,
                     what='attribute', name='some_data', obj=0))
        expected = """data with ``a : in code`` and :ref:`reference` and no type"""

        self.assertEqual(expected, actual)

    def test_class_data_member_inline_ref_in_type(self):
        config = Config()
        docstring = """:class:`int`: data member description"""
        actual = str(GoogleDocstring(docstring, config=config, app=None,
                     what='attribute', name='some_data', obj=0))
        expected = dedent("""\
        data member description

        :type: :class:`int`""")

        extensions = ['sphinx.ext.napoleon']

        napoleon_include_private_with_doc = False

        napoleon_use_admonition_for_notes = False

    .. _NumPy style:

    ----------

        for Google style docstrings.
    napoleon_numpy_docstring : :obj:`bool` (Defaults to True)

        'napoleon_include_private_with_doc': (False, 'env'),

def setup(app: Sphinx) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Sphinx extension setup function.

    When the extension is loaded, Sphinx imports this module and executes
    the ``setup()`` function, which in turn notifies Sphinx of everything
    the extension offers.

    Parameters
    ----------
    app : sphinx.application.Sphinx
        Application object representing the Sphinx process

    See Also
    --------
    `The Sphinx documentation on Extensions
    <http://sphinx-doc.org/extensions.html>`_

    `The Extension Tutorial <http://sphinx-doc.org/extdev/tutorial.html>`_

    `The Extension API <http://sphinx-doc.org/extdev/appapi.html>`_

    app.setup_extension('sphinx.ext.autodoc')

def _patch_python_domain() -> None:
    try:
        from sphinx.domains.python import PyTypedField
    except ImportError:
        pass
    else:
        import sphinx.domains.python
        from sphinx.locale import _
        for doc_field in sphinx.domains.python.PyObject.doc_field_types:
            if doc_field.name == 'parameter':
                doc_field.names = ('param', 'parameter', 'arg', 'argument')
                break

            PyTypedField('keyword', label=_('Keyword Arguments'),

def _process_docstring(app: Sphinx, what: str, name: str, obj: Any,
                       options: Any, lines: List[str]) -> None:
    """Process the docstring for a given python object.

    Called when autodoc has read and processed a docstring. `lines` is a list
    of docstring lines that `_process_docstring` modifies in place to change
    what Sphinx outputs.

    The following settings in conf.py control what styles of docstrings will
    be parsed:

    * ``napoleon_google_docstring`` -- parse Google style docstrings
    * ``napoleon_numpy_docstring`` -- parse NumPy style docstrings

    Parameters
    ----------
    app : sphinx.application.Sphinx
        Application object representing the Sphinx process.
    what : str
        A string specifying the type of the object to which the docstring
        belongs. Valid values: "module", "class", "exception", "function",
        "method", "attribute".
    name : str
        The fully qualified name of the object.
    obj : module, class, exception, function, method, or attribute
        The object to which the docstring belongs.
    options : sphinx.ext.autodoc.Options
        The options given to the directive: an object with attributes
        inherited_members, undoc_members, show_inheritance and noindex that
        are True if the flag option of same name was given to the auto
        directive.
    lines : list of str
        The lines of the docstring, see above.

        .. note:: `lines` is modified *in place*

    """
    result_lines = lines
    docstring = None  # type: GoogleDocstring
    if app.config.napoleon_numpy_docstring:
        docstring = NumpyDocstring(result_lines, app.config, app, what, name,
                                   obj, options)
        result_lines = docstring.lines()
    if app.config.napoleon_google_docstring:
        docstring = GoogleDocstring(result_lines, app.config, app, what, name,
                                    obj, options)
        result_lines = docstring.lines()
    lines[:] = result_lines[:]

    The following settings in conf.py determine if private and special class
    members or init methods are included in the generated documentation:

    * ``napoleon_include_init_with_doc`` --
      include init methods if they have docstrings
    * ``napoleon_include_private_with_doc`` --
      include private members if they have docstrings
    * ``napoleon_include_special_with_doc`` --
      include special members if they have docstrings

    Parameters
    ----------
    app : sphinx.application.Sphinx
        Application object representing the Sphinx process
    what : str
        A string specifying the type of the object to which the member
        belongs. Valid values: "module", "class", "exception", "function",
        "method", "attribute".
    name : str
        The name of the member.
    obj : module, class, exception, function, method, or attribute.
        For example, if the member is the __init__ method of class A, then
        `obj` will be `A.__init__`.
    skip : bool
        A boolean indicating if autodoc will skip this member if `_skip_member`
        does not override the decision
    options : sphinx.ext.autodoc.Options
        The options given to the directive: an object with attributes
        inherited_members, undoc_members, show_inheritance and noindex that
        are True if the flag option of same name was given to the auto
        directive.

    Returns
    -------
    bool
        True if the member should be skipped during creation of the docs,
        False if it should be included in the docs.

    """
    has_doc = getattr(obj, '__doc__', False)
    is_member = (what == 'class' or what == 'exception' or what == 'module')
    if name != '__weakref__' and has_doc and is_member:
        cls_is_owner = False
        if what == 'class' or what == 'exception':
            qualname = getattr(obj, '__qualname__', '')
            cls_path, _, _ = qualname.rpartition('.')
            if cls_path:
                try:
                    if '.' in cls_path:
                        import importlib
                        import functools

                        mod = importlib.import_module(obj.__module__)
                        mod_path = cls_path.split('.')
                        cls = functools.reduce(getattr, mod_path, mod)
                    else:
                        cls = obj.__globals__[cls_path]
                except Exception:
                    cls_is_owner = False
                else:
                    cls_is_owner = (cls and hasattr(cls, name) and  # type: ignore
                                    name in cls.__dict__)
            else:
                cls_is_owner = False

        if what == 'module' or cls_is_owner:
            is_init = (name == '__init__')
            is_special = (not is_init and name.startswith('__') and
                          name.endswith('__'))
            is_private = (not is_init and not is_special and
                          name.startswith('_'))
            inc_init = app.config.napoleon_include_init_with_doc
            inc_special = app.config.napoleon_include_special_with_doc
            inc_private = app.config.napoleon_include_private_with_doc
            if ((is_special and inc_special) or
                    (is_private and inc_private) or
                    (is_init and inc_init)):
                return False
    return None

  These three are explicit hardcoded escape sequences. The trailing-underscore mapping encodes the assumption that all occurrences of a trailing underscore should be escaped with a backslash when generating RST.

Instead I inspected usage sites: Napoleon is wired into Sphinx via sphinx/ext/napoleon/__init__.py, which creates NumpyDocstring and/or GoogleDocstring instances and replaces the docstring lines in the autodoc pipeline (_process_docstring). The final lines produced by the docstring parser (a list of RST lines) are taken by Sphinx autodoc and then parsed/rendered into HTML. So the RST emitted by Napoleon must be syntactically correct and compatible with Sphinx's directive parsing & rendering.

Diagnosis summary before proposing fixes:
- Napoleon's _escape_args_and_kwargs escapes trailing underscores (hello_ -> hello\_) for RST safety. That escape is emitted into RST both for field roles (:ivar:) and for directives (.. attribute::). In at least one path (.. attribute::) Sphinx appears to render the backslash literally (in the final HTML), causing visible backslashes. The test suite in this repository expects the RST returned by the docstring parser to include the backslash when ivar is used, which is fine for that context (Sphinx unescapes/handles that in the role rendering). However, when that exact escaped string is used as the argument of the attribute directive, Sphinx seems to treat it as literal text (and the backslash is not interpreted as escape), leading to a visible backslash in HTML.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (high level)
- Keep the escaping behavior for argument names in general (this preserves existing behavior for :param:, :ivar:, :type:, etc., and preserves test expectations in test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py).
- But when emitting directive arguments (i.e., building the literal directive lines '.. attribute:: <name>' and '.. method:: <name>'), unescape the trailing underscore escape that _escape_args_and_kwargs added (replace r'\_' with '_'), because attribute/method directive arguments should contain the raw trailing underscore and Sphinx handles them properly; leaving r'\_' there causes the backslash to be visible in HTML.

Specifically change these call sites in sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:
1) In GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section
   - Original snippet:
       lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name)
   - Change to:
       directive_name = _name.replace(r'\_', '_')
       lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + directive_name)

2) In GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section
   - Original snippet:
       lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name)
   - Change to:
       method_name = _name.replace(r'\_', '_')
       lines.append('.. method:: %s' % method_name)

(Optionally, handle other directive-emission sites where escaped name is placed directly into a directive heading, but in this file the two sites above are the relevant ones.)

Concrete patch (conceptual)
- In sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:
  - At the attribute directive emission:
    replace:
      lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name)
    with:
      lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))
  - At the method directive emission:
    replace:
      lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name)
    with:
      lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))

Now the required checklist evaluations:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The code originally assumed that escaping trailing underscores by inserting r'\_' is appropriate for all output contexts; my fix changes the assumption to: directive arguments (e.g., the '.. attribute::' and '.. method::' directive headers) should use raw names (no backslash-escaped trailing underscore), while other contexts (inline roles and field names) still benefit from the escape.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The hardcoded escape values from _escape_args_and_kwargs:
  - r'\_' for trailing underscore
  - r'\*\*' and r'\*' for leading '**' and '*' respectively
- If we remove the assumption (i.e., we no longer want r'\_' to be present in directive headings), the value r'\_' is not correct for all output paths — specifically, it is not correct when directly inserted into directive arguments. Therefore instead of changing the constant itself globally, we selectively undo it for directive argument emission. The other hardcoded escapes (r'\*', r'\*\*') remain okay because they may still be desirable where Sphinx would otherwise treat '*' as markup. If we later find out that leading-star backslashes also appear literally for directive arguments, we would need to apply the same unescape for those cases. At present, the only problematic hardcoded value is r'\_' when used in directive arguments.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse (reading) operation here is Sphinx parsing RST emitted by Napoleon. We must ensure that by unescaping the directive arguments, Sphinx will parse them correctly and not treat e.g. trailing underscore as a reference target or other markup. I examined the places where the parent class (none for GoogleDocstring) or other inherited methods perform complementary operations; in this case the main complementary operation is Sphinx's later parsing of the directive argument when generating HTML. The net substitutions:
  - For attribute directive: input to Sphinx before fix: ".. attribute:: hello\_" (Rst with backslash). Sphinx produced HTML with visible backslash (bad).
  - After fix: ".. attribute:: hello_" (raw underscore). Sphinx will parse the directive argument normally and produce HTML showing "hello_" without backslash (correct).
- Trace concrete variable values for relevant parent methods: GoogleDocstring does not have a parent that further post-processes directive strings; Sphinx does the final parsing. The change is safe: directive arguments are plain text and should contain the name as-is. Tests that check RST-level output for :ivar: remain unchanged.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Effects on methods in GoogleDocstring:
  - _escape_args_and_kwargs unchanged — still escapes trailing underscores for use in field roles like :param: or :ivar:, so behavior for those contexts remains unchanged.
  - _parse_attributes_section now emits raw names in the attribute directive header, which fixes the visible-backslash bug. The subsequent fields (':type:' lines, body) still use the escaped names where appropriate because we did not change the names used there.
  - _parse_methods_section now similarly emits raw method names in '.. method::' headers. Everything else continues to work.
- No other methods rely on the attribute directive having backslash escapes. The tests that assert RST strings containing escaped underscored names (e.g., the test_underscore_in_attribute expecting ':ivar arg\\_: ...' when napoleon_use_ivar=True) will remain unaffected because that path still uses the escaped name.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required. We only alter the emitted string in two call sites to undo the escape for directive contexts. There is no need to add overrides or new behavior in parent classes.

(Concrete method-by-method verification)
- Method: _escape_args_and_kwargs
  - Effect: unchanged. Keeps escaping trailing underscore and leading star(s).
  - Works: Yes — still needed for inline roles.
- Method: _parse_attributes_section
  - Key variables: _name (escaped by _escape_args_and_kwargs), _type, _desc
  - Computation before: outputs '.. attribute:: ' + _name (i.e. '.. attribute:: hello\_')
  - Computation after: outputs '.. attribute:: ' + _name.replace(r'\_', '_') (i.e. '.. attribute:: hello_')
  - Expected: the attribute directive should show hello_ in HTML without extraneous backslash. Override needed? Already in this method; no new method required.
- Method: _parse_methods_section
  - Similar to attributes: method header will emit raw method name.
- All other methods: verified not to emit escaped names into directive headers; no changes needed.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (root cause and complete fix locations)

Root cause
- Napoleon's docstring parser intentionally escapes trailing underscores by inserting r'\_' (in GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs) to avoid accidental Sphinx/reST interpretation when names appear inline (fields/roles, e.g., :param: or :ivar:).
- The escaped name was also used verbatim as the argument for directive headers (e.g., '.. attribute:: <escaped-name>' and '.. method:: <escaped-name>'). For these directives, Sphinx does not treat the backslash as escape to be removed in the same way as in field roles, so the backslash became visible in rendered HTML.
- In short: over-escaping (and using the escaped string verbatim for directive arguments) led to a literal backslash in the rendered HTML.

Every location that needs to change (and why)
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py
   - _parse_attributes_section (around the lines that build the attribute directive)
     - Current behavior: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name) where _name can contain r'\_'.
     - Problem: r'\_' appears literally in directive argument and leads to visible backslash in HTML.
     - Fix: unescape backslash-underscore before putting into the directive argument:
         lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))
   - _parse_methods_section (around lines that build the method directive)
     - Current behavior: lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name) where _name may contain r'\_'.
     - Problem: same as attribute directive.
     - Fix: unescape trailing underscore similarly:
         lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))

Rationale for not changing _escape_args_and_kwargs globally
- _escape_args_and_kwargs is used in many contexts. Removing the r'\_' escape there would change other behavior (e.g., :ivar:, :param: fields) and could break tests or expected reST output where escaping is desired. The minimal and correct fix is to emit raw names specifically in directive argument positions (where backslash is shown literally), while keeping the escapes for inline roles/fields.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- The only hardcoded value implicated is r'\_' present in _escape_args_and_kwargs. I do not recommend making it dynamic; instead selectively undo it in the directive contexts as described. No other hardcoded numeric constants are relevant to this bug.

Any methods to add or override
- No new methods are necessary. We only change two places where directive-arguments are constructed.

Why this is safe / why tests should still pass
- Tests that assert the RST produced by the docstring parser (e.g., tests expecting ':ivar arg\\_: ...' when napoleon_use_ivar=True) remain unchanged because we did not modify the escaping in inline roles.
- The change only unescapes the trailing underscore when used as the argument of the attribute/method directives — exactly the contexts that led to visible backslashes in HTML. Sphinx's directive parsing will render the raw underscore correctly in HTML.

Suggested code patch (summary)
- In sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:
  - Replace the attribute directive emission:
      lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name)
    with:
      lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name.replace(r'\\_', '_'))
    (in the actual file use the raw Python string replacement: _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))
  - Replace the method directive emission:
      lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name)
    with:
      lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name.replace(r'\_', '_'))

Notes / extra considerations
- If other directive headings are added later that take escaped names as arguments, the same unescaping treatment should be applied.
- If tests are added or existing behavior relies on the escaped underscores showing up literally in directive argument strings at the RST stage, those tests should be updated; however, based on the bug report and typical reST behavior, directive arguments are the right place to use raw names.

If you want, I can:
- Produce an exact unified diff/patch for the two changes (the precise line numbers depend on the file version but I can prepare a patch).
- Run through additional searches to ensure there are no other directive-emission sites in Napoleon or elsewhere that insert escaped names verbatim (I already searched docstring.py and found the two relevant sites).
- Expand the unescape to also handle leading-star escapes if you prefer (i.e., .replace(r'\*', '*')), but I avoided that because it may be unnecessary and could affect parameter signature rendering.

Would you like the precise patch/diff to apply to sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py now?
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: Napoleon emits escaped trailing underscores (e.g. hello_ -> hello\_) into directive arguments (like ".. attribute:: hello\_"), and the backslash becomes visible in the generated HTML. The wrong assumption is "escape trailing underscores everywhere is safe".
- Root cause & location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py — GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs injects r'\_' for trailing underscores; that escaped value is used verbatim when emitting directive headers in GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section and GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section.
- Fix strategy: keep the existing escape logic for inline roles/fields (preserve _escape_args_and_kwargs), but undo (replace r'\_' → '_') right before inserting names into directive headers (".. attribute::" and ".. method::"). This is a minimal, targeted change and covers the visible-backslash symptom without changing other RST output expected by tests.
- Hardcoded values: the literal escapes r'\_' (trailing underscore), r'\*' and r'\*\*' in _escape_args_and_kwargs encode the previous global assumption. Only r'\_' needs to be selectively undone for directive arguments; do not remove it globally.
- Methods to add/override: none required. Only two emission sites need string replacement; no new API or methods are necessary.
- Investigation gaps: I did not run a full Sphinx build to validate complex downstream parsing/rendering of every directive type, and I did not exhaustively scan for other directive-emission sites outside docstring.py that may also be placing escaped names verbatim into directive headers (though I searched this file and found the two relevant sites). I also did not test leading '*' escape interactions in directive arguments (rare).

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Over-escaping occurs in _escape_args_and_kwargs (root cause)
What: GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs turns trailing underscore into r'\_' (adds a literal backslash). This encodes the assumption "always escape trailing underscores".  
Why it matters: that escaped name is propagated into directive headers and becomes visible in HTML.  
Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs  
Found at: checkpoint 2 (file read) and checkpoint 1 (initial search)

### Finding 2: Directive headers use the escaped name verbatim (symptom point)
What: GoogleDocstring inserts the escaped _name directly into directive header strings: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name) and lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name), so r'\_' survives into RST directive arguments.  
Why it matters: Sphinx renders those directive arguments literally (the backslash is not removed), producing visible backslashes in HTML.  
Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section and ::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section  
Found at: checkpoint 2 (file read) and checkpoint 3 (pattern scan)

### Finding 3: Tests show escaped underscore is expected for inline fields — don't remove global escape
What: tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py includes expectations like ":ivar arg\\_: some description" and ":vartype arg\\_: type" (the RST-level representation includes r'\_').  
Why it matters: globally removing r'\_' from _escape_args_and_kwargs would break expected RST output in inline field contexts; so the fix must be targeted to directive headers only.  
Location: tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py (test_underscore_in_attribute around lines ~1392–1404)  
Found at: checkpoint 1 (test inspection)

### Finding 4: Minimal safe fix is to unescape only before directive emission
What: Replace r'\_' with '_' only when constructing directive arguments (attribute/method headers). This preserves inline-field escaping while eliminating visible backslashes in HTML for directives.  
Why it matters: addresses the symptom precisely with minimal surface-area change.  
Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py — modify the two emission points in _parse_attributes_section and _parse_methods_section.  
Found at: checkpoint 4 (proposed fix analysis)

### Finding 5: Gaps and additional checks
What: Did not run a full Sphinx rendering pass for other directive types or check other extensions that might also use escaped names verbatim. Did not check leading-star escapes (r'\*' / r'\*\*') for directive header visibility.  
Why it matters: similar visible-backslash problems might exist for other escapes or other directive contexts; follow-up testing recommended.  
Found at: checkpoint 4 (verification reasoning)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs
- Wrong assumption: "Trailing underscores must be escaped (r'\_') in all emitted RST contexts."
- Evidence:
  - Implementation (exact behavior): in _escape_args_and_kwargs the code does:
    - if name.endswith('_'): name = name[:-1] + r'\_'
    - if name[:2] == '**': return r'\*\*' + name[2:]
    - if name[:1] == '*': return r'\*' + name[1:]
  - Search/trace shows this method is called in _consume_field and other places that produce names used later by directive emissions.
  - Found during file inspection (docstring.py lines around 320–327 in the checked version).
- Related locations:
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._consume_field (calls _escape_args_and_kwargs)
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::NumpyDocstring._consume_field (overrides/also calls it)

### Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section
- Wrong assumption: "It is safe to insert the escaped name returned by _escape_args_and_kwargs directly into a directive header (.. attribute:: <name>)."
- Evidence:
  - Code emits: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name) where _name may be e.g. 'hello\\_' (literal backslash).
  - User reproduction: produced '.. attribute:: hello\_' in generated RST which produced visible backslash in HTML output.
  - Tests show ':ivar arg\\_:' is expected for inline fields, so this directive-specific use is the unique problematic site.
  - Found during file inspection and pattern search (docstring.py around where attributes are parsed/emitted, ~line 588 in inspected version).
- Related locations:
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section (same pattern)
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._format_fields / inline field emitters (use escaped names correctly and must remain unchanged)

### Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py:GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section
- Wrong assumption: "Method directive headers can safely embed escaped names (.. method:: <name>)."
- Evidence:
  - Code emits: lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name) and will place r'\_' into the header if present, leading to visible backslash in HTML.
  - Found during file inspection (docstring.py around method emission, ~line 648 in inspected version).
- Related locations:
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section (same fix needed)
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs (source of the escaped text)

### Location: tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py: (test_underscore_in_attribute)
- Wrong assumption (in potential naive fix): "We can remove all r'\_' escapes globally without breaking tests."
- Evidence:
  - The test asserts expected RST includes escaped underscores for inline fields:
    - expected contains: ":ivar arg\\_: some description" and ":vartype arg\\_: type"
    - This demonstrates some contexts require r'\_' to be present in the RST string.
  - Found during test inspection (around lines 1392–1404 in the checked test file).
- Related locations:
  - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::_escape_args_and_kwargs (must remain to satisfy this test)

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify the diagnostic claims in the draft enhanced report against the actual source and tests. I explain below which files and locations I inspected, why, what I expected to learn, and what the code shows.

Tool use summary (what I fetched and why)
- I read sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py to inspect implementations of:
  - GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs — to confirm whether trailing underscores are escaped and how.
  - GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field — to confirm callers of the escape function and propagation of escaped names.
  - GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section and _parse_methods_section — to confirm if escaped names are inserted verbatim into directive headers.
  - Other formatting methods that consume _name to check whether some contexts expect escaped names.
  Expected: find the escape function, its callers, and the emission sites that place names into directive headers.
- I opened tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py around the test that checks underscores in attributes to confirm tests expect escaped underscores in some inline-field contexts.
- I searched the tree for occurrences of the literal escape sequence r'\_' and for the directive emission patterns to confirm the set of affected locations.

What the code and tests show (detailed verification)

1) GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs
- What I inspected: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py around the escape function.
- Key lines found:
  - Line 320–323: 
    if name.endswith('_'):
        name = name[:-1] + r'\_'
  - Line 324–329:
    if name[:2] == '**':
        return r'\*\*' + name[2:]
    elif name[:1] == '*':
        return r'\*' + name[1:]
    else:
        return name
- Verification: The draft's claim that _escape_args_and_kwargs unconditionally converts a trailing underscore into r'\_' is accurate. The function also always prefixes leading '*' or '**' with backslash-escaped forms. The code unconditionally returns the escaped string to callers.

2) GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field (callers)
- What I inspected:
  - GoogleDocstring._consume_field: lines 228–249 show it reads a line, partitions it, optionally parses a typed arg, and then calls _escape_args_and_kwargs at line 241: _name = self._escape_args_and_kwargs(_name)
  - NumpyDocstring._consume_field: in the NumpyDocstring subclass, at line 887 it also calls _escape_args_and_kwargs: _name = self._escape_args_and_kwargs(_name)
- Verification: Both GoogleDocstring and NumpyDocstring consume names from docstring fields and pass those names through _escape_args_and_kwargs before returning them. Thus escaped names propagate from parsing into the returned field tuples used by later emission/formatting code.

3) GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section and _parse_methods_section (emission sites)
- What I inspected:
  - _parse_attributes_section: line 580–600 shows two code paths:
    - if napoleon_use_ivar is True: code constructs ':ivar %s:' entries with _name (inline-field style).
    - else (default path): code emits lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name) at line 588 (directive header insertion).
  - _parse_methods_section: line 645–654 contains lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name) at line 648.
- Verification: These emission sites insert _name verbatim into RST directive headers (.. attribute::, .. method::). Since _name may contain literal backslashes (for trailing underscores or leading '*'), the backslash characters are placed directly into the produced RST directive arguments.

4) Tests that expect escaped underscores in inline-field contexts
- What I inspected: tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py around test_underscore_in_attribute (lines ~1392–1409).
- Key lines found:
  - expected = """
    :ivar arg\\_: some description
    :vartype arg\\_: type
    """
- Verification: Tests explicitly assert that inline-field output (when napoleon_use_ivar=True) includes the escaped underscore (r'\_') for attribute names ending in underscore. This demonstrates that the escaping behavior is required for some contexts (inline RST fields) and thus cautions against removing the escape globally.

5) Root-cause consistency
- The draft states that the root cause is the global application of escaping (in _escape_args_and_kwargs) and use of the escaped string verbatim for directive headers.
- Evidence from the code:
  - Escape is applied at a centralized function and invoked by both GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field.
  - Emission sites for directive headers use the parsed _name values verbatim (.. attribute:: and .. method::).
- Verification: The code supports the stated root cause: the same escaped name string (with r'\_') is appropriate in inline-field RST contexts but is also used unchanged in directive headers, where the backslash becomes visible in HTML.

6) Additional relevant locations and completeness
- NumpyDocstring also calls the escape function (confirmed) and therefore is subject to the same propagation pattern.
- Formatting functions that produce inline fields (e.g., _format_docutils_params and _format_fields) also consume _name values and are intended to generate inline :param/:type/:ivar constructs — contexts where r'\_' is expected and tests assert that behavior.
- I searched for occurrences of r'\_' and found the escape in docstring.py and uses in tests; no other directive-emission sites outside _parse_attributes_section and _parse_methods_section in docstring.py were found that insert the parsed _name into directive headers. The primary problematic emission sites are the two directive header insertions confirmed above.

7) Claims in the draft that require softening or correction
- The draft included a "fix strategy" and described a specific code change (undo r'\_' only before directive emission). Because the reviewer agent must produce a diagnosis-only report, any prescriptive or implementation guidance must be removed from RootCause and other fields. The code evidence supports the diagnosis (where and why backslashes appear), but not the inclusion of any proposed code changes in the report fields.
- The draft's RootCause listed many correct observations but omitted explicit reference to NumpyDocstring._consume_field as an additional caller that propagates the escape; that must be added so all related locations with the same root cause get equal weight.

8) Field-discipline / content corrections required
- RootCause must be factual and limited to "what is wrong and where" — it should not include remediation suggestions or sequences of steps that look like a patch plan.
- Description, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior should be concise and user-visible only (no implementation details).
- StepsToReproduce in the draft are accurate and remain fine (they are reproduction steps).

Summary of verification results (what I will change in the revised report)
- Keep Title and Description, but ensure wording is concise and factual.
- Rewrite RootCause to:
  - State precisely that _escape_args_and_kwargs unconditionally escapes trailing underscores (quote lines).
  - State that both GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field call that function and thus propagate escaped names.
  - State that GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section and GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section insert these escaped names verbatim into directive headers (quote lines).
  - State that inline-field formatters (e.g., _format_docutils_params / inline :ivar/:vartype) do expect r'\_' (supported by test), so the escape cannot be removed globally without test breakage.
  - List all affected locations with equal weight: _escape_args_and_kwargs, GoogleDocstring._consume_field, NumpyDocstring._consume_field, _parse_attributes_section, _parse_methods_section, and mention the inline-field formatters/tests that depend on the escape.
- Remove any fix prescriptions from the RootCause and other fields.
- Keep StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior but ensure they are concise and implementation-free.
- Provide an evidence list pointing to the specific file/line references and the test expectations.

bug_report.Title: Napoleon emits escaped trailing underscore in directive headers (visible backslash in HTML)
bug_report.Description: When using sphinx.ext.napoleon, attribute and method names that end with a trailing underscore (for example `hello_`) are emitted into directive headers as `hello\_`, producing a visible backslash in the generated HTML.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs unconditionally replaces a trailing underscore with an escaped form. The implementation contains: if name.endswith('_'): name = name[:-1] + r'\_' and also returns escaped leading-star forms (r'\*', r'\**'). This means parsed names for fields may contain literal backslash characters.

2) Both GoogleDocstring and NumpyDocstring parse field names via their _consume_field implementations and call _escape_args_and_kwargs on the parsed name. Evidence: GoogleDocstring._consume_field calls _escape_args_and_kwargs(_name) (docstring.py) and NumpyDocstring._consume_field does the same in the subclass. As a result, the escaped names produced by _escape_args_and_kwargs propagate in the parsed field tuples returned to later formatting/emission code.

3) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section inserts the parsed (already-escaped) name directly into an attribute directive header in one code path: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name). When _name contains r'\_' this backslash is placed verbatim into the directive argument.

4) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section likewise inserts the parsed name directly into a method directive header: lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name). An escaped trailing underscore in _name therefore appears verbatim in the directive argument.

5) Inline-field formatters and tests demonstrate that some RST contexts expect the escaped underscore. For example, when napoleon_use_ivar is True, attribute output is emitted as ':ivar <name>:' and the test test_underscore_in_attribute expects ':ivar arg\\_:' and ':vartype arg\\_:' in the produced RST. Thus the escaping behavior is required for inline-field contexts, and the same escaped string is both correct in some places and problematic when inserted into directive headers.

Affected locations (equal weight, listed):
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs (escape implementation)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._consume_field (caller that applies the escape)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::NumpyDocstring._consume_field (caller that applies the escape)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section (emits '.. attribute:: <name>')
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section (emits '.. method:: <name>')
- tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py::test_underscore_in_attribute (documents expected escaped underscore in inline-field output)

Note: This diagnosis identifies the mismatch between a global escaping policy (applied at parse time) and contexts where that escaped text is later used verbatim in directive headers, producing visible backslashes in rendered HTML. It does not prescribe a remediation.
StepsToReproduce: Create an empty __init__.py

Create a.py containing:

class A:
    """
    Attributes
    ----------
    hello_: int
        hi
    """
    pass
Run sphinx-quickstart to create a docs build
In conf.py add 'sphinx.ext.autodoc' and 'sphinx.ext.napoleon' to extensions
Add '.. autoclass:: a.A' to index.rst
Run (from project root) PYTHONPATH=. make clean html
Open _build/html/index.html in a browser and inspect the attribute name
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Attribute and method names ending with an underscore appear in the generated HTML as the plain name with trailing underscore (e.g. 'hello_') without a visible backslash.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Attribute and method names ending with an underscore are rendered in HTML with a visible backslash before the underscore (e.g. 'hello\\_').

"""
    test_environment_indexentries
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

    Test the sphinx.environment.managers.indexentries.

    :copyright: Copyright 2007-2020 by the Sphinx team, see AUTHORS.
    :license: BSD, see LICENSE for details.
"""

import pytest

from sphinx.environment.adapters.indexentries import IndexEntries
from sphinx.testing import restructuredtext


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_single_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: docutils\n"
            ".. index:: Python\n"
            ".. index:: pip; install\n"
            ".. index:: pip; upgrade\n"
            ".. index:: Sphinx\n"
            ".. index:: Ель\n"
            ".. index:: ёлка\n"
            ".. index:: ‏תירבע‎\n"
            ".. index:: 9-symbol\n"
            ".. index:: &-symbol\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 6
    assert index[0] == ('Symbols', [('&-symbol', [[('', '#index-9')], [], None]),
                                    ('9-symbol', [[('', '#index-8')], [], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('D', [('docutils', [[('', '#index-0')], [], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('P', [('pip', [[], [('install', [('', '#index-2')]),
                                            ('upgrade', [('', '#index-3')])], None]),
                              ('Python', [[('', '#index-1')], [], None])])
    assert index[3] == ('S', [('Sphinx', [[('', '#index-4')], [], None])])
    assert index[4] == ('Е', [('ёлка', [[('', '#index-6')], [], None]),
                               ('Ель', [[('', '#index-5')], [], None])])
    assert index[5] == ('ת', [('‏תירבע‎', [[('', '#index-7')], [], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_pair_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: pair: docutils; reStructuredText\n"
            ".. index:: pair: Python; interpreter\n"
            ".. index:: pair: Sphinx; documentation tool\n"
            ".. index:: pair: Sphinx; :+1:\n"
            ".. index:: pair: Sphinx; Ель\n"
            ".. index:: pair: Sphinx; ёлка\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 7
    assert index[0] == ('Symbols', [(':+1:', [[], [('Sphinx', [('', '#index-3')])], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('D',
                        [('documentation tool', [[], [('Sphinx', [('', '#index-2')])], None]),
                         ('docutils', [[], [('reStructuredText', [('', '#index-0')])], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('I', [('interpreter', [[], [('Python', [('', '#index-1')])], None])])
    assert index[3] == ('P', [('Python', [[], [('interpreter', [('', '#index-1')])], None])])
    assert index[4] == ('R',
                        [('reStructuredText', [[], [('docutils', [('', '#index-0')])], None])])
    assert index[5] == ('S',
                        [('Sphinx', [[],
                                     [(':+1:', [('', '#index-3')]),
                                      ('documentation tool', [('', '#index-2')]),
                                      ('ёлка', [('', '#index-5')]),
                                      ('Ель', [('', '#index-4')])],
                                     None])])
    assert index[6] == ('Е', [('ёлка', [[], [('Sphinx', [('', '#index-5')])], None]),
                               ('Ель', [[], [('Sphinx', [('', '#index-4')])], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_triple_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: triple: foo; bar; baz\n"
            ".. index:: triple: Python; Sphinx; reST\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 5
    assert index[0] == ('B', [('bar', [[], [('baz, foo', [('', '#index-0')])], None]),
                              ('baz', [[], [('foo bar', [('', '#index-0')])], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('F', [('foo', [[], [('bar baz', [('', '#index-0')])], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('P', [('Python', [[], [('Sphinx reST', [('', '#index-1')])], None])])
    assert index[3] == ('R', [('reST', [[], [('Python Sphinx', [('', '#index-1')])], None])])
    assert index[4] == ('S', [('Sphinx', [[], [('reST, Python', [('', '#index-1')])], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_see_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: see: docutils; reStructuredText\n"
            ".. index:: see: Python; interpreter\n"
            ".. index:: see: Sphinx; documentation tool\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 3
    assert index[0] == ('D', [('docutils', [[], [('see reStructuredText', [])], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('P', [('Python', [[], [('see interpreter', [])], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('S', [('Sphinx', [[], [('see documentation tool', [])], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_seealso_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: seealso: docutils; reStructuredText\n"
            ".. index:: seealso: Python; interpreter\n"
            ".. index:: seealso: Sphinx; documentation tool\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 3
    assert index[0] == ('D', [('docutils', [[], [('see also reStructuredText', [])], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('P', [('Python', [[], [('see also interpreter', [])], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('S', [('Sphinx', [[], [('see also documentation tool', [])], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_main_index(app):
    text = (".. index:: !docutils\n"
            ".. index:: docutils\n"
            ".. index:: pip; install\n"
            ".. index:: !pip; install\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)
    assert len(index) == 2
    assert index[0] == ('D', [('docutils', [[('main', '#index-0'),
                                             ('', '#index-1')], [], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('P', [('pip', [[], [('install', [('main', '#index-3'),
                                                         ('', '#index-2')])], None])])


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_index_with_name(app):
    text = (".. index:: single: docutils\n"
            "   :name: ref1\n"
            ".. index:: single: Python\n"
            "   :name: ref2\n"
            ".. index:: Sphinx\n")
    restructuredtext.parse(app, text)
    index = IndexEntries(app.env).create_index(app.builder)

    # check index is created correctly
    assert len(index) == 3
    assert index[0] == ('D', [('docutils', [[('', '#ref1')], [], None])])
    assert index[1] == ('P', [('Python', [[('', '#ref2')], [], None])])
    assert index[2] == ('S', [('Sphinx', [[('', '#index-0')], [], None])])

    # check the reference labels are created correctly
    std = app.env.get_domain('std')
    assert std.anonlabels['ref1'] == ('index', 'ref1')
    assert std.anonlabels['ref2'] == ('index', 'ref2')


@pytest.mark.sphinx('dummy', freshenv=True)
def test_create_index_by_key(app):
    # At present, only glossary directive is able to create index key
    text = (".. glossary::\n"
            "\n"
            "   docutils\n"
            "   Python\n"
            "   スフィンクス : ス\n")

"""
    sphinx.domains.index
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

    The index domain.

    :copyright: Copyright 2007-2020 by the Sphinx team, see AUTHORS.
    :license: BSD, see LICENSE for details.
"""

from typing import Any, Dict, Iterable, List, Tuple

from docutils import nodes
from docutils.nodes import Node, system_message
from docutils.parsers.rst import directives

from sphinx import addnodes
from sphinx.domains import Domain
from sphinx.environment import BuildEnvironment
from sphinx.util import logging
from sphinx.util import split_index_msg
from sphinx.util.docutils import ReferenceRole, SphinxDirective
from sphinx.util.nodes import process_index_entry

if False:
    # For type annotation
    from sphinx.application import Sphinx


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class IndexDomain(Domain):
    """Mathematics domain."""
    name = 'index'
    label = 'index'

    @property
    def entries(self) -> Dict[str, List[Tuple[str, str, str, str, str]]]:
        return self.data.setdefault('entries', {})

    def clear_doc(self, docname: str) -> None:
        self.entries.pop(docname, None)

    def merge_domaindata(self, docnames: Iterable[str], otherdata: Dict) -> None:
        for docname in docnames:
            self.entries[docname] = otherdata['entries'][docname]

    def process_doc(self, env: BuildEnvironment, docname: str, document: Node) -> None:
        """Process a document after it is read by the environment."""
        entries = self.entries.setdefault(env.docname, [])
        for node in document.traverse(addnodes.index):
            try:
                for entry in node['entries']:
                    split_index_msg(entry[0], entry[1])
            except ValueError as exc:
                logger.warning(str(exc), location=node)
                node.parent.remove(node)
            else:
                for entry in node['entries']:
                    entries.append(entry)


class IndexDirective(SphinxDirective):
    """
    Directive to add entries to the index.
    """
    has_content = False
    required_arguments = 1
    optional_arguments = 0
    final_argument_whitespace = True
    option_spec = {
        'name': directives.unchanged,
    }

    def run(self) -> List[Node]:
        arguments = self.arguments[0].split('\n')

        if 'name' in self.options:
            targetname = self.options['name']
            targetnode = nodes.target('', '', names=[targetname])
        else:
            targetid = 'index-%s' % self.env.new_serialno('index')
            targetnode = nodes.target('', '', ids=[targetid])

        self.state.document.note_explicit_target(targetnode)
        indexnode = addnodes.index()
        indexnode['entries'] = []
        indexnode['inline'] = False
        self.set_source_info(indexnode)
        for entry in arguments:
            indexnode['entries'].extend(process_index_entry(entry, targetnode['ids'][0]))
        return [indexnode, targetnode]


class IndexRole(ReferenceRole):
    def run(self) -> Tuple[List[Node], List[system_message]]:
        target_id = 'index-%s' % self.env.new_serialno('index')
        if self.has_explicit_title:
            # if an explicit target is given, process it as a full entry
            title = self.title
            entries = process_index_entry(self.target, target_id)
        else:
            # otherwise we just create a single entry
            if self.target.startswith('!'):
                title = self.title[1:]
                entries = [('single', self.target[1:], target_id, 'main', None)]
            else:
                title = self.title
                entries = [('single', self.target, target_id, '', None)]

        index = addnodes.index(entries=entries)
        target = nodes.target('', '', ids=[target_id])
        text = nodes.Text(title, title)
        self.set_source_info(index)
        return [index, target, text], []


def setup(app: "Sphinx") -> Dict[str, Any]:
    app.add_domain(IndexDomain)
    app.add_directive('index', IndexDirective)
    app.add_role('index', IndexRole())

    return {
        'version': 'builtin',
        'env_version': 1,
        'parallel_read_safe': True,
        'parallel_write_safe': True,
    }

Tool call: get_file_context for sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py (full file)
Result summary / analysis:
- I read the full create_index implementation. Important points:
  - Entries are collected into a dict 'new' keyed by the index word.
  - Entries are sorted for same keyword (keyfunc0).
  - Then index entries are sorted globally by keyfunc, which computes a lowercased normalized key (lckey).
  - In keyfunc, there's this logic:

    if lckey[0:1].isalpha() or lckey.startswith('_'):
        lckey = chr(127) + lckey

    and a comment: "put all symbols at the front, even those following the letters in ASCII, this is where the chr(127) comes from"

  - Later keyfunc2 (for subentries) uses the same chr(127) prefix for alphabetic/underscore keys.
  - Finally keyfunc3 groups entries by letter; when the first character is not a letter or '_' it returns _('Symbols').

- The create_index returns [(key_, list(group)) for (key_, group) in groupby(newlist, keyfunc3)]

Reasoning / initial diagnosis from reading:
- The code attempts to put symbols at the front by prefixing letters with chr(127) so symbols (ASCII symbols like '@') sort before letters.
- But chr(127) only sits at codepoint 127; many Unicode symbols have codepoints > 127 (for example '£' U+00A3 = 163, arrows like '←' U+2190 = 8592). Those will compare greater than chr(127) and thus be sorted after letters. That explains the bug described: some symbols (ASCII ones, e.g. '@' U+0040) have codepoints < 127 and thus appear before letters; other Unicode symbols with codepoints >127 appear after letters; groupby then creates two separate groups both labeled 'Symbols' (since keyfunc3 returns _('Symbols') for both), producing duplicate headings and duplicate anchors/links (same anchor string used twice), leading to the observed behavior.

Files/methods primarily responsible:
- sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py::IndexEntries.create_index is primarily responsible for sorting and grouping logic that leads to two 'Symbols' sections.
- Consumers of the create_index output: sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py::StandaloneHTMLBuilder.write_genindex uses the create_index result to create the genindex page(s). Duplicate keys produce duplicate headings / duplicate per-key pages in the index and duplicate anchor usage in templates.

Checkpoint 1 conclusion:
- The root cause is the sorting sentinel used in keyfunc / keyfunc2: chr(127) is insufficient — it only works for ASCII ranges and not for full Unicode. As a result, some symbols fall before letters and some after, producing two separate 'Symbols' groups.
